## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017 - "Attention Is All You Need")**
> A Transformer is a deep learning architecture that relies entirely on a **self-attention mechanism** to compute representations of its inputs and outputs, eliminating recurrence and convolutions in favor of parallelizable matrix operations. It forms the backbone of modern Generative AI models such as **GPT**, **BERT**, **T5**, **LLaMA**, and **Stable Diffusion's text encoder**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PERUT756 – Generative AI, Module 2), the Transformer is positioned as the *core generative architecture* that replaced earlier RNN/LSTM-based sequence models. It is a **sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) model** composed of an **encoder stack** and a **decoder stack**, each built from identical layers containing **Multi-Head Self-Attention** and **Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks**, with residual connections and layer normalization.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Spotlight at a Conference":**
> Imagine you are attending a crowded international conference with 100 speakers talking simultaneously in different languages. An RNN is like a translator who must listen to speaker 1, then 2, then 3 — one ear at a time, in order, slowly losing track of speaker 1's context.
>
> A **Transformer**, by contrast, is like a person with **100 adjustable spotlights**. At every moment, each spotlight can independently swivel to focus on whichever speaker is most relevant to the current translation task, regardless of where that speaker is sitting. The *brightness* of each spotlight is the **attention weight**. This is exactly how a Transformer attends to all input tokens in **parallel** rather than sequentially.

### 1.3 Why Transformers Matter in Generative AI

- **Parallelization**: Unlike RNNs, training can be fully parallelized on GPUs/TPUs.
- **Long-range dependencies**: Directly connects any two tokens via a single attention step (path length **O(1)**), vs. **O(n)** in RNNs.
- **Scalability**: Foundation for large language models (LLMs) with **billions of parameters**.
- **Versatility**: Same architecture powers text generation, image generation (Vision Transformer), audio (Whisper), and multimodal models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Terminology for KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Token** — smallest unit of input (word/sub-word).
> - **Embedding** — dense vector representation of a token.
> - **Context vector** — output of attention, encoding relationships with all other tokens.
> - **Query (Q), Key (K), Value (V)** — three projections derived from input embeddings.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Transformer Block

A single Transformer layer executes the following steps in order:

1. **Input Embedding** — Convert each token $x_i$ into a dense vector of dimension $d_{\text{model}}$.
2. **Positional Encoding** — Add sinusoidal or learned position vectors (since attention is permutation-invariant without it).
3. **Project to Q, K, V** — Multiply input by three learned weight matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V$.
4. **Compute Scaled Dot-Product Attention** — Score relevance of every token against every other token.
5. **Apply Softmax** — Normalize scores to a probability distribution summing to 1.
6. **Weighted Sum of Values** — Multiply attention weights by Value vectors.
7. **Residual Connection + LayerNorm** — Stabilize gradients (Add & Norm).
8. **Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — Two linear transformations with ReLU/GELU in between.
9. **Residual Connection + LayerNorm** — Second Add & Norm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Scaled" Dot-Product?**
> Without scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$, dot products grow in magnitude with embedding dimension, pushing softmax into regions with near-zero gradients (saturation). Division by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps variance stable.

### 2.2 Self-Attention: The Core "Why" and "How"

**Why self-attention?**
- Captures **contextual relationships** between all tokens in a sequence simultaneously.
- Each token's new representation is a *weighted combination* of all tokens, weighted by relevance.

**How does it work?**
- Each token produces a **Query** (what am I looking for?), a **Key** (what do I contain?), and a **Value** (what information do I carry?).
- Compatibility between tokens is measured by the dot product $Q \cdot K^T$.
- Softmax turns scores into attention probabilities; values are mixed accordingly.

### 2.3 Multi-Head Attention (MHA)

Instead of performing a single attention function, the Transformer projects Q, K, V into $h$ different learned linear subspaces (heads), runs attention in parallel, and concatenates the outputs. This allows the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Core attention operation; $d_k$ = key dimension |
| 2 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ | Each head: $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ |
| 3 | Per-head dimension | $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h$ | Standard setting: $h = 8$, $d_{\text{model}} = 512$ |
| 4 | Positional Encoding (sinusoidal) | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | Even indices |
| 5 | Positional Encoding (sinusoidal) | $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$ | Odd indices |
| 6 | Position-wise FFN | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Applied independently to each position |
| 7 | Layer Normalization | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \cdot \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Stabilizes training; computed across features |
| 8 | Residual Connection | $y = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$ | Enables training of very deep networks |
| 9 | Masked Attention (Decoder) | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V$ | $M$ masks future tokens with $-\infty$ |
| 10 | Number of parameters per layer (approx) | $\approx 4 d_{\text{model}}^2$ (for Q,K,V,O projections) | Excludes FFN and embeddings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Board:**
> The denominator $\sqrt{d_k}$ is **mandatory** in the formula. Writing $QK^\top V$ alone is incomplete and will cost marks. Always write the **full scaled form**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **NLP**: Machine translation, summarization, chatbots (GPT, Gemini).
- **Computer Vision**: Vision Transformer (ViT) for image classification.
- **Speech**: Whisper, Conformer (ASR).
- **Multimodal Generative AI**: DALL-E, Stable Diffusion (CLIP text encoder).
- **Code generation**: GitHub Copilot, Code Llama.
- **Scientific discovery**: AlphaFold 2/3 (protein structure) uses attention-based architectures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Self-Attention Output (Single Head)

**Given:**
- Input sequence $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$ with $n$ tokens and embedding dimension $d_{\text{model}}$.
- Learnable weight matrices $W^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$.

**Step 1 — Project inputs into Query, Key, and Value matrices.**

$$
Q = X \, W^Q
$$

$$
K = X \, W^K
$$

$$
V = X \, W^V
$$

**Explanation:** Each row of $Q$ (resp. $K$, $V$) is the query (resp. key, value) vector of one token, produced by a learned linear projection.

---

**Step 2 — Compute raw similarity scores via dot product.**

$$
S = Q \, K^{\top}
$$

Here $S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, and entry $S_{ij}$ measures how strongly token $i$ attends to token $j$.

**Explanation:** A dot product is large when the query of token $i$ aligns directionally with the key of token $j$ — i.e., token $j$ is relevant to token $i$.

---

**Step 3 — Scale to control variance.**

$$
S' = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Explanation:** Since $Q$ and $K$ entries are roughly zero-mean unit-variance, the variance of each entry of $S$ is $d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores unit variance, preventing softmax saturation.

---

**Step 4 — Apply softmax row-wise to obtain attention weights.**

$$
A = \text{softmax}(S') \quad \text{where} \quad A_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp(S'_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(S'_{ik})}
$$

**Explanation:** Softmax normalizes each row so that the attention weights for token $i$ sum to 1, forming a probability distribution over all tokens.

---

**Step 5 — Compute the weighted sum of Values.**

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A \, V
$$

**Explanation:** The output for token $i$ is a convex combination of all value vectors, weighted by how relevant each source token is to it.

---

**Final compact expression:**

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Multi-Head Attention Output

**Step 1 — Split into $h$ heads with reduced dimension.**

$$
d_k = d_v = \dfrac{d_{\text{model}}}{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Independent projections per head.**

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}\!\left(X W_i^Q,\; X W_i^K,\; X W_i^V\right), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, h
$$

**Step 3 — Concatenate all heads.**

$$
\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h \cdot d_v} = \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final linear projection.**

$$
\text{MHA}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O
$$

where $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}}$.

**Explanation:** Different heads learn to focus on different relationship types — one head may attend to syntactic dependencies, another to coreference, another to positional neighbors — and the output projection $W^O$ recombines them into a unified representation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Complexity Comparison (Frequently Asked)

| Architecture | Sequential Ops | Max Path Length | Parallelization |
|---|---|---|---|
| RNN / LSTM | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Limited (sequential) |
| CNN (kernel $k$) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ (parallel) | $\mathcal{O}(\log_k n)$ | High |
| **Self-Attention (Transformer)** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | **Full** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Path length** = number of sequential operations required to relate two distant positions. Lower path length ⇒ easier to learn long-range dependencies. This is a **high-yield KTU point**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Transformer Architecture (Encoder-Decoder)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["Input Tokens"] --> embA["Token Embedding"]
    embA --> peA["Positional Encoding Addition"]
    peA --> encA["Encoder Stack N times"]
    encA --> encOut["Encoder Memory K and V"]

    encOut --> decAttn["Decoder Cross Attention"]
    inputB["Shifted Right Output Tokens"] --> embB["Token Embedding"]
    embB --> peB["Positional Encoding Addition"]
    peB --> decMasked["Masked Self Attention"]
    decMasked --> decAttn
    decAttn --> decFFN["Decoder Feed Forward and LayerNorm"]
    decFFN --> decA["Decoder Stack N times"]
    decA --> linear["Linear Projection"]
    linear --> softmaxOut["Softmax over Vocabulary"]
    softmaxOut --> probs["Output Probability Distribution"]
```

### 4.2 Internal Structure of a Single Transformer Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inpNode["Input x"] --> attnNode["Multi Head Self Attention"]
    attnNode --> addNorm1["Add and LayerNorm"]
    inpNode --> addNorm1
    addNorm1 --> ffnNode["Position wise Feed Forward Network"]
    ffnNode --> addNorm2["Add and LayerNorm"]
    addNorm1 --> addNorm2
    addNorm2 --> outNode["Output to Next Layer"]
```

### 4.3 Scaled Dot-Product Attention Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    qNode["Query Matrix Q"] --> matMul["Q times K Transpose"]
    kNode["Key Matrix K"] --> matMul
    matMul --> scaleNode["Divide by sqrt dk"]
    scaleNode --> maskNode["Optional Mask"]
    maskNode --> smNode["Softmax"]
    smNode --> weightedSum["Weighted Sum"]
    vNode["Value Matrix V"] --> weightedSum
    weightedSum --> attnOut["Attention Output"]
```

### 4.4 Multi-Head Attention Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    xIn["Input X"] --> split["Linear Projections into h subspaces"]
    split --> h1["Head 1 Attention"]
    split --> h2["Head 2 Attention"]
    split --> h3["Head 3 Attention"]
    split --> hN["Head h Attention"]
    h1 --> concat["Concatenate All Heads"]
    h2 --> concat
    h3 --> concat
    hN --> concat
    concat --> wO["Final Linear Projection W O"]
    wO --> mhaOut["Multi Head Output"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define self-attention mechanism. Why is the dot product scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$ in Transformer architecture?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Self-attention is a mechanism that computes a weighted representation of every token in a sequence by relating it to all other tokens in the same sequence. Given input embeddings $X$, it produces three projections — Query $Q = XW^Q$, Key $K = XW^K$, and Value $V = XW^V$ — and computes:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

The dot product is scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$ because as $d_k$ grows, the magnitude of $QK^{\top}$ entries grows proportionally, pushing the softmax into saturation regions where gradients are vanishingly small. Scaling restores unit variance and stabilizes training. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between self-attention and cross-attention as used in the Transformer decoder.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention |
|---|---|---|
| Source of Q, K, V | All three from the **same** sequence | **Q** from decoder; **K, V** from encoder output |
| Purpose | Capture intra-sequence dependencies | Link decoder state to encoder context |
| Used in | Both encoder and decoder (decoder uses masked variant) | Decoder only |
| Masking | Decoder self-attention is causal-masked | No masking — full encoder context available |

In self-attention, queries, keys, and values all derive from the same input, while in cross-attention, the queries come from the decoder's previous layer but the keys and values come from the encoder's output memory. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module-2 questions offer an internal choice between two questions (Q9 vs Q10, etc.). Both options are presented below.

---

### Question A — (14 Marks) `[KTU ESE Model – 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** mechanism in detail with neat mathematical formulation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Scaled Dot-Product Attention is the fundamental attention operation used in the Transformer. Given input token embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$, the mechanism proceeds as follows.

**Step 1:** Generate Query, Key, and Value matrices using learnable projection matrices $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$ and $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$.

$$
Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V
$$

**Step 2:** Compute pairwise similarity scores by matrix multiplication:

$$
S = Q K^{\top} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

**Step 3:** Scale the scores to prevent softmax saturation:

$$
S' = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply softmax along rows to obtain attention weight matrix $A$:

$$
A_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp(S'_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(S'_{ik})}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the weighted sum of values:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A V
$$

**Combined formula:**

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

**Significance:** Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is essential because dot products of zero-mean unit-variance vectors have variance $d_k$, leading to extremely peaked softmax distributions for large $d_k$. The mechanism allows each token to dynamically attend to all other tokens in the sequence in parallel, with a maximum path length of $\mathcal{O}(1)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Describe the **Multi-Head Attention** mechanism. How does it improve over single-head attention? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Multi-Head Attention (MHA) runs $h$ parallel self-attention "heads" on different learned projections of the input, allowing the model to jointly attend to information from $h$ different representation subspaces.

**Step 1 — Project into $h$ heads.** For each head $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, h\}$, define learnable projections $W_i^Q, W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$ and $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$ where $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h$.

**Step 2 — Compute each head independently.**

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}\!\left(X W_i^Q,\; X W_i^K,\; X W_i^V\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Concatenate all heads.**

$$
\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2, \ldots, \text{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final linear projection.**

$$
\text{MHA}(X) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) \, W^O
$$

where $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}}$.

**Improvements over single-head attention:**
1. **Multi-faceted relationships:** Different heads can specialize in different syntactic or semantic relations (subject-verb agreement, coreference, positional proximity).
2. **Stable training:** Reduced per-head dimension ($d_k = d_{\text{model}}/h$) keeps computation comparable to single-head attention while increasing representational capacity.
3. **Richer representations:** Joint attention to information from multiple subspaces produces richer contextual embeddings.
4. **Empirical gain:** In the original Transformer paper, multi-head attention outperformed single-head attention on translation BLEU scores. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) `[KTU ESE Model – 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **Transformer encoder block** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of residual connections and layer normalization. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A Transformer encoder block consists of two sub-layers, each wrapped with a residual connection and layer normalization.

**Sub-Layer 1 — Multi-Head Self-Attention:**

$$
z_1 = \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(x + \text{MHA}(x)\right)
$$

Here $x$ is the input embedding (with positional encoding added). The output $z_1$ is a context-aware representation of the same dimensionality.

**Sub-Layer 2 — Position-wise Feed-Forward Network:**

$$
z_2 = \text{LayerNorm}\!\left(z_1 + \text{FFN}(z_1)\right)
$$

The FFN is two linear transformations with a non-linearity (typically ReLU or GELU):

$$
\text{FFN}(u) = \max(0,\, u W_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2
$$

**Block Diagram (textual):**
Input $x$ → Multi-Head Self-Attention → Add & Norm → Feed-Forward Network → Add & Norm → Output

**Role of Residual Connections:**
- Mitigate the **vanishing gradient problem** in very deep networks (e.g., 96-layer Transformers).
- Allow gradients to flow directly through the skip path, preserving the original signal.
- Effectively let each sub-layer learn a *residual function* $F(x) = \text{Sublayer}(x)$, making optimization easier.

**Role of Layer Normalization:**
- Normalizes activations across the feature dimension at each position independently.
- Stabilizes hidden state dynamics, accelerates convergence.
- Reduces internal covariate shift, allowing higher learning rates.
- Unlike batch norm, layer norm is independent of batch size — crucial for variable-length sequences. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Why are **positional encodings** required in Transformers? Derive the sinusoidal positional encoding formulas and state their key properties. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Why Positional Encoding is Required:**
Since self-attention is **permutation-invariant** — i.e., it treats input as an unordered set — the Transformer has no inherent notion of token order. "The cat sat on the mat" and "mat the on sat cat the" would produce identical attention outputs. Positional encodings inject order information by adding a position-dependent vector to each token embedding.

**Sinusoidal Positional Encoding Formulas:**

For position $pos$ and embedding dimension index $i$:

$$
PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{\,2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

$$
PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{\,2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

**Explanation of Design Choices:**
- Different frequencies $1/10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}$ are used for different dimensions, allowing the model to attend to *relative* positions via linear combinations.
- The wavelength forms a geometric progression from $2\pi$ to $10000 \cdot 2\pi$.
- Each dimension corresponds to a sinusoid of a particular frequency.

**Key Properties:**
1. **Unique:** Every position gets a distinct encoding vector.
2. **Bounded:** Values lie in $[-1, 1]$, well-scaled with embeddings.
3. **Relative position expressible:** $PE_{pos+k}$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE_{pos}$ using the trigonometric identity $\sin(a+b) = \sin a \cos b + \cos a \sin b$, enabling the model to learn relative offsets.
4. **Extrapolation:** Generalizes to sequence lengths longer than those seen in training.
5. **Deterministic:** No additional parameters to learn (though learned positional embeddings are an alternative used in GPT, BERT). **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner's Perspective):**
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling factor** $\sqrt{d_k}$ in the attention formula — worth **1 mark** minimum. Always write the *full* scaled form.
> 2. **Confusing Q, K, V sources** — in self-attention, ALL three come from the same input $X$; in cross-attention, only $Q$ comes from the decoder.
> 3. **Omitting residual connections or layer norm** in the encoder block diagram — examiners specifically check for the "Add & Norm" wrapper around each sub-layer.
> 4. **Writing only the FFN formula without ReLU/GELU** — non-linearity is mandatory.
> 5. **Mixing up encoder/decoder roles** — encoder has bi-directional self-attention; decoder has *masked* self-attention plus cross-attention. Diagrams must show both.
> 6. **Defining positional encoding without mentioning permutation invariance** — the *reason* for its use is mandatory.
> 7. **Not specifying dimensions** of matrices (e.g., $W^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$) — KTU board values dimensional correctness.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Transformer = encoder-decoder stack using **only** attention (no recurrence, no convolution).
- [ ] Core operation: **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** $\text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k})V$.
- [ ] **Q (Query)** = what the token is looking for; **K (Key)** = what the token contains; **V (Value)** = what information the token provides.
- [ ] **Multi-Head Attention** = $h$ parallel attention heads, then concatenated and projected by $W^O$.
- [ ] Each encoder layer: MHA → Add&Norm → FFN → Add&Norm.
- [ ] Each decoder layer: Masked MHA → Add&Norm → Cross-Attention → Add&Norm → FFN → Add&Norm.
- [ ] **Positional encoding** is mandatory because attention is permutation-invariant.
- [ ] **Sinusoidal PE** uses $\sin$ for even indices and $\cos$ for odd indices with geometric frequency progression.
- [ ] **Residual connections** solve vanishing gradients; **Layer Norm** stabilizes activations.
- [ ] **Path length** in self-attention = $\mathcal{O}(1)$ vs. $\mathcal{O}(n)$ in RNNs — explains superior long-range modeling.
- [ ] **Masked attention** in decoder prevents attending to future tokens (causal/autoregressive property).
- [ ] **Feed-Forward Network** is applied **position-wise** (independently to each token), with inner dimensionality typically $4 \times d_{\text{model}}$.
- [ ] Standard hyperparameters: $h = 8$ heads, $d_{\text{model}} = 512$, $d_k = d_v = 64$, $N = 6$ encoder/decoder layers.
- [ ] Transformers are the foundation of **GPT** (decoder-only), **BERT** (encoder-only), and **T5** (encoder-decoder) — all of which power modern Generative AI.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attention Mechanism in Detail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT756 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Attention Mechanism in Detail

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attention Mechanism (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The Attention Mechanism is a computational framework in deep learning that enables a neural network to dynamically weight the importance of different elements in an input sequence when producing an output. It computes a weighted sum of *Value* representations, where the weights are determined by the compatibility between a *Query* and the corresponding *Key* vectors. This allows the model to "attend" selectively to the most relevant parts of the input, overcoming the fixed-context bottleneck of recurrent architectures.

In the context of **Generative AI**, attention is the foundational building block of the **Transformer** architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017), enabling large language models (LLMs) such as GPT, BERT, and T5 to model long-range dependencies with **parallel computation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Search

Imagine you are researching a topic in a vast library. You don't read every book sequentially; instead:

- You formulate a **Query** (your research question).
- Each book in the library has a **Title (Key)**.
- You compare your query against titles to compute a **Relevance Score (Attention Weight)**.
- You then pull out the actual **Content (Value)** from the most relevant books, weighted by their scores.

The Attention Mechanism does exactly this — but in a high-dimensional vector space, using **dot products** and **softmax normalization**.

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Vectors

| Vector | Symbol | Role | Library Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Query (Q)** | $Q$ | The element seeking information | Your research question |
| **Key (K)** | $K$ | The labels used for matching | Book titles on the shelf |
| **Value (V)** | $V$ | The actual content retrieved | Book contents |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In **Self-Attention**, Q, K, and V are all derived from the *same* input sequence using three different learned linear projections. In **Cross-Attention**, Q comes from one sequence (e.g., decoder) while K and V come from another (e.g., encoder).

## 1.4 Historical Evolution

| Year | Milestone | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| 2014 | Bahdanau Attention | First additive attention for seq2seq translation |
| 2015 | Luong Attention | Introduced dot-product (multiplicative) attention |
| 2017 | Transformer | Multi-head scaled dot-product self-attention |
| 2018–Present | GPT, BERT, T5 | Attention scales to foundation models with **billions of parameters** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Framework — Scaled Dot-Product Attention

The canonical formulation (Vaswani et al., 2017) is given by:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

Where:
- $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ — matrix of $n$ query vectors of dimension $d_k$
- $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_k}$ — matrix of $m$ key vectors of dimension $d_k$
- $V \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_v}$ — matrix of $m$ value vectors of dimension $d_v$
- $d_k$ — dimensionality of keys (used as a **scaling factor**)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1 — Compute Compatibility Scores
Each query is compared with every key via a dot product, yielding a raw score matrix of shape $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$:

$$S = QK^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \cdot k_1 & q_1 \cdot k_2 & \cdots & q_1 \cdot k_m \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ q_n \cdot k_1 & q_n \cdot k_2 & \cdots & q_n \cdot k_m \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$
Raw dot products grow in **magnitude** with dimension $d_k$, pushing the softmax into regions of vanishing gradients. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to approximately **1**.

### Step 3 — Apply Softmax (Row-wise)
Convert raw scores into a valid probability distribution over keys for each query:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_{ij}/\sqrt{d_k})}{\sum_{k=1}^{m}\exp(S_{ik}/\sqrt{d_k})}$$

These $\alpha_{ij}$ are the **attention weights**.

### Step 4 — Weighted Sum over Values
Each output is the attention-weighted combination of values:

$$\text{Output}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m}\alpha_{ij}\,V_j$$

## 2.3 Multi-Head Attention

Rather than performing a single attention function, the Transformer projects Q, K, V into **$h$ different learned subspaces** and runs attention in parallel:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\,W^O$$

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q,\; KW_i^K,\; VW_i^V)$$

Where the projection matrices satisfy:
- $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$
- Typically $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h$

> [!TIP]
> **Why multiple heads?** Each head can attend to information from a different representation subspace — one head might capture syntactic dependencies, another semantic role, another positional proximity. This multi-perspective analysis is the source of Transformer's expressive power.

## 2.4 Types of Attention (KTU Classification)

| Type | Computation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-Attention** | Q, K, V from same sequence | Encoder/Decoder internal |
| **Cross-Attention** | Q from decoder, K, V from encoder | Seq2Seq translation |
| **Causal (Masked) Attention** | Upper triangle of score matrix set to $-\infty$ | Autoregressive generation (GPT) |
| **Bidirectional Attention** | Full score matrix visible | BERT-style encoders |
| **Bahdanau (Additive)** | $\tanh(W_q q + W_k k)$ in a feed-forward net | RNN-based seq2seq |
| **Luong (Multiplicative)** | $q^{\top} W k$ or $q^{\top} k$ | Faster dot-product variant |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = QK^{\top}$ | Raw compatibility score matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| 2 | $S_{\text{scaled}} = \dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ | Variance-normalized scores | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| 3 | $\alpha = \text{softmax}(S_{\text{scaled}})$ | Attention weight matrix (rows sum to **1**) | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| 4 | $\text{Out} = \alpha V$ | Final attended representation | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ |
| 5 | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ | Multi-head concatenation | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{\text{model}}}$ |
| 6 | $\text{Var}(q_i k_j) = d_k$ | Variance of raw dot products | scalar |
| 7 | $\text{CausalMask}(S)_{ij} = \begin{cases} S_{ij}, & j \le i \\ -\infty, & j > i \end{cases}$ | Mask for autoregressive models | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Machine Translation:** Google Translate, DeepL.
- **Document Summarization:** Long-context LLMs (Claude, Gemini).
- **Computer Vision:** Vision Transformers (ViT) for image classification.
- **Protein Folding:** AlphaFold 2 uses **Evoformer blocks** built on axial attention.
- **Speech & Audio:** Whisper (OpenAI) uses cross-attention for transcription alignment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$?

Assume the components of $q$ and $k$ are independent random variables with **zero mean** and **unit variance**:

$$\mathbb{E}[q_i] = \mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0, \quad \text{Var}(q_i) = \text{Var}(k_i) = 1$$

We derive the variance of the dot product $s = q^{\top} k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$.

**Step 1 — Compute the expectation.**

$$\mathbb{E}[s] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i k_i]$$

Since $q_i$ and $k_i$ are independent with zero mean:

$$\mathbb{E}[q_i k_i] = \mathbb{E}[q_i]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbb{E}[s] = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance.**

By independence of the components:

$$\text{Var}(s) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i)$$

For independent variables:

$$\text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2 k_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[q_i k_i])^2 = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] = \text{Var}(q_i)\cdot\text{Var}(k_i) = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} 1 = d_k$$

**Step 3 — Standard deviation and scaling.**

$$\text{Std}(s) = \sqrt{d_k}$$

Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ yields a scaled score $\tilde{s} = s/\sqrt{d_k}$ with:

$$\mathbb{E}[\tilde{s}] = 0, \quad \text{Var}(\tilde{s}) = 1$$

This keeps the softmax inputs in a region of well-behaved gradients — **not too peaked** (which would saturate softmax) and **not too flat** (which would wash out distinctions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Without scaling, when $d_k = 512$, dot products have standard deviation $\sqrt{512} \approx 22.6$, pushing softmax into saturation. The factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores numerical stability — this is precisely why the original Transformer paper called it **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Softmax Gradient Stability Argument

Let $\beta = 1/\sqrt{d_k}$ be the scaling factor applied before softmax. We can analyze the effect on the Jacobian of softmax:

$$\text{softmax}(x_i) = \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}$$

The Jacobian element is:

$$\frac{\partial\,\text{softmax}(x_i)}{\partial x_j} = \text{softmax}(x_i)\left(\delta_{ij} - \text{softmax}(x_j)\right)$$

For large $\vert x_i - x_j \vert$ (i.e., large $d_k$ unscaled), $\text{softmax}(x_i) \to 1$ for the maximum and $\to 0$ for others, causing the gradient of $\text{softmax}(x_i)$ w.r.t. $x_i$ to approach **zero**. This is the **vanishing gradient problem**. Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the input differences moderate, preserving non-trivial gradients.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given 3 tokens with the following Query, Key, Value vectors (each of dimension $d_k = 2$), compute the scaled dot-product attention output.

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Raw scores.**

$$S = QK^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 1+0\cdot 0 & 1\cdot 0+0\cdot 1 & 1\cdot 1+0\cdot 1 \\ 0\cdot 1+1\cdot 0 & 0\cdot 0+1\cdot 1 & 0\cdot 1+1\cdot 1 \\ 1\cdot 1+1\cdot 0 & 1\cdot 0+1\cdot 1 & 1\cdot 1+1\cdot 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.**

$$S_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 0.707 & 0.707 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & 1.414 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Row-wise softmax.**

For Row 1: $[0.707, 0, 0.707]$

$$\exp(\text{row}) = [e^{0.707}, e^{0}, e^{0.707}] \approx [2.028, 1.000, 2.028]$$

$$\alpha_1 = \left[\frac{2.028}{5.056},\;\frac{1.000}{5.056},\;\frac{2.028}{5.056}\right] \approx [0.401, 0.198, 0.401]$$

For Row 2: $[0, 0.707, 0.707]$ → by symmetry, $\alpha_2 \approx [0.198, 0.401, 0.401]$.

For Row 3: $[0.707, 0.707, 1.414]$

$$\exp(\text{row}) = [2.028, 2.028, 4.113]$$

$$\alpha_3 = \left[\frac{2.028}{8.169},\;\frac{2.028}{8.169},\;\frac{4.113}{8.169}\right] \approx [0.248, 0.248, 0.504]$$

**Step 4 — Final attention matrix.**

$$\alpha \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.401 & 0.198 & 0.401 \\ 0.198 & 0.401 & 0.401 \\ 0.248 & 0.248 & 0.504 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Each row sums to **1.000** ✓)

**Step 5 — Output = $\alpha V$.**

For Output 1:

$$\text{Out}_1 = 0.401 \cdot [1,0] + 0.198 \cdot [0,1] + 0.401 \cdot [1,1]$$

$$\text{Out}_1 = [0.401+0.401,\; 0.198+0.401] = [0.802,\; 0.599]$$

By symmetry, $\text{Out}_2 = [0.599, 0.802]$.

For Output 3:

$$\text{Out}_3 = 0.248 \cdot [1,0] + 0.248 \cdot [0,1] + 0.504 \cdot [1,1]$$

$$\text{Out}_3 = [0.248+0.504,\; 0.248+0.504] = [0.752,\; 0.752]$$

**Final Output Matrix.**

$$\text{Output} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.802 & 0.599 \\ 0.599 & 0.802 \\ 0.752 & 0.752 \end{bmatrix}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Bahdanau (Additive) | Luong (Dot) | Scaled Dot-Product (Transformer) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Score Function** | $v^{\top}\tanh(W_q q + W_k k)$ | $q^{\top} k$ or $q^{\top} W k$ | $q^{\top} k / \sqrt{d_k}$ |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ |
| **Parallelizable** | No (RNN-bound) | Yes | **Yes** |
| **Gradient Stability** | Good | Risk for large $d_k$ | **Excellent (with scaling)** |
| **Used In** | Original seq2seq (2014) | Luong et al. (2015) | All modern Transformers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scaled Dot-Product Attention Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInQ["Input Queries Q"] --> nodeMul1["Q times K Transpose"]
    nodeInK["Input Keys K"] --> nodeKT["K Transpose"]
    nodeKT --> nodeMul1
    nodeMul1 --> nodeScale["Divide by sqrt dk"]
    nodeScale --> nodeMask["Optional Mask"]
    nodeMask --> nodeSoft["Softmax"]
    nodeSoft --> nodeWeights["Attention Weights alpha"]
    nodeWeights --> nodeMul2["Weighted Sum"]
    nodeInV["Input Values V"] --> nodeMul2
    nodeMul2 --> nodeOut["Output"]
```

## 4.2 Multi-Head Attention Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIn["Input X"] --> nodeProjQ["Linear WQ"]
    nodeIn --> nodeProjK["Linear WK"]
    nodeIn --> nodeProjV["Linear WV"]
    nodeProjQ --> nodeSplit["Split into h heads"]
    nodeProjK --> nodeSplit
    nodeProjV --> nodeSplit
    nodeSplit --> nodeHead1["Head 1 Attention"]
    nodeSplit --> nodeHead2["Head 2 Attention"]
    nodeSplit --> nodeHead3["Head h Attention"]
    nodeHead1 --> nodeConcat["Concatenate"]
    nodeHead2 --> nodeConcat
    nodeHead3 --> nodeConcat
    nodeConcat --> nodeOutProj["Linear WO"]
    nodeOutProj --> nodeOut["Multi Head Output"]
```

## 4.3 Self-Attention vs Cross-Attention (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENC["Encoder Side"]
        nodeEncIn["Encoder Hidden States"]
        nodeEncK["K from Encoder"]
        nodeEncV["V from Encoder"]
        nodeEncIn --> nodeEncK
        nodeEncIn --> nodeEncV
    end
    subgraph DEC["Decoder Side"]
        nodeDecIn["Decoder Hidden State"]
        nodeDecQ["Q from Decoder"]
        nodeDecIn --> nodeDecQ
    end
    nodeDecQ --> nodeAttn["Cross Attention Module"]
    nodeEncK --> nodeAttn
    nodeEncV --> nodeAttn
    nodeAttn --> nodeDecOut["Decoder Output Token"]
    subgraph SELF["Self Attention Inside Decoder"]
        nodeSelf1["Q K V from Decoder"]
        nodeSelf1 --> nodeMasked["Masked Attention"]
        nodeMasked --> nodeSelfOut["Context Vector"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Complete Transformer Block Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeEmb["Token + Positional Embedding"] --> nodeMHA1["Multi Head Self Attention"]
    nodeMHA1 --> nodeAddNorm1["Add and LayerNorm"]
    nodeAddNorm1 --> nodeFFN["Feed Forward Network"]
    nodeFFN --> nodeAddNorm2["Add and LayerNorm"]
    nodeAddNorm2 --> nodeOut["Output to Next Block"]
```

## 4.5 Causal Masking Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1["Token 1"] --> nodeMask["Causal Mask Matrix"]
    nodeT2["Token 2"] --> nodeMask
    nodeT3["Token 3"] --> nodeMask
    nodeT4["Token 4"] --> nodeMask
    nodeMask --> nodeMat["Lower Triangular Visible"]
    nodeMat --> nodeResult["Token 4 attends to Tokens 1 2 3 4 only"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define the **attention mechanism** and explain the role of Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors in it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The attention mechanism is a neural network component that computes a weighted combination of input elements, where the weights are dynamically derived from the input itself. It uses three projections:
- **Query (Q):** Represents the current element seeking context.
- **Key (K):** Acts as an indexable label for each input element.
- **Value (V):** Carries the actual content to be aggregated.

The output is computed as $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$, allowing the model to focus on the most relevant input positions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
What is **scaled dot-product attention**, and why is the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ necessary?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Scaled dot-product attention computes compatibility scores as $QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k}$ before applying softmax. The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ is required because the dot products of $d_k$-dimensional vectors have variance $d_k$, causing softmax inputs to become large in magnitude. This pushes softmax into saturation, leading to **vanishing gradients**. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance to 1, ensuring stable training.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Derive the variance of the dot product $q^{\top} k$ assuming $q$ and $k$ are independent zero-mean unit-variance random vectors of dimension $d_k$. Hence justify the use of the scaling factor $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ in scaled dot-product attention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the architecture of **multi-head attention**. With neat diagrams, show how $h$ parallel attention heads are projected, concatenated, and combined. What is the advantage of using multiple heads over a single attention head? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Variance Derivation** — See **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.

**Summary of expected steps:**
1. State assumptions: $\mathbb{E}[q_i] = \mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0$, $\text{Var}(q_i) = \text{Var}(k_i) = 1$.
2. Show $\mathbb{E}[q_i k_i] = 0$ by independence.
3. Compute $\text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] = 1$.
4. Sum over $i = 1, \dots, d_k$: $\text{Var}(q^{\top} k) = d_k$.
5. Conclude that scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ brings variance to 1, stabilizing softmax.

**(b) Multi-Head Attention Explanation** — See **Section 2.3** and the diagram in **Section 4.2**.

**Key points to write:**
- Project Q, K, V using $W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V$ into $h$ subspaces of dimension $d_{\text{model}}/h$.
- Run $h$ parallel scaled dot-product attention operations.
- Concatenate outputs and apply final projection $W^O$.
- **Advantages:** captures different relationship types (syntactic, semantic, positional), increases model expressiveness without proportional cost (total parameters roughly equal to single-head full-dim attention).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Differentiate between **Bahdanau (additive) attention** and **Luong (dot-product) attention**. Provide mathematical formulations and discuss their computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A self-attention layer receives an input matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$ as follows. Compute the scaled dot-product attention output, taking $Q = XW_Q$, $K = XW_K$, $V = XW_V$, with:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad W_Q = W_K = W_V = I_{2 \times 2}$$

Use $d_k = 2$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bahdanau vs Luong:**

| Criterion | Bahdanau (Additive) | Luong (Dot) |
|---|---|---|
| Score Function | $e_{ij} = v^{\top}\tanh(W_q h_i^{\text{dec}} + W_k h_j^{\text{enc}})$ | $e_{ij} = h_i^{\top} h_j$ (or $h_i^{\top} W h_j$) |
| Mechanism | Feed-forward alignment network | Pure dot product |
| Complexity | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ |
| Parallelism | Limited (depends on RNN) | **Fully parallelizable** |
| Year | 2014 | 2015 |

**(b) Numerical Computation** — This is exactly the **Section 3.3** worked example. Follow those steps to obtain:

$$\text{Output} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.802 & 0.599 \\ 0.599 & 0.802 \\ 0.752 & 0.752 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling factor:** Writing $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top})V$ instead of $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$. Examiners deduct **at least 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Q, K, V:** Mixing up which vector is used for what — Query for *seeking*, Key for *matching*, Value for *content*.
> 3. **Skipping softmax justification:** Not mentioning that softmax converts scores to a probability distribution (rows sum to 1).
> 4. **Wrong softmax row/column:** Applying softmax along the wrong axis — must be row-wise (each query attends to all keys).
> 5. **No mention of vanishing gradient:** Forgetting to link scaling to gradient stability. Worth **2 marks** in derivation questions.
> 6. **Multi-head parameter confusion:** Stating total parameters scale with $h$ — they don't (each head uses $d_{\text{model}}/h$ dimensions, keeping total cost constant).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Attention** is a weighted-sum mechanism where weights are computed from the input itself.
- Three core vectors: **Query (Q)**, **Key (K)**, **Value (V)**.
- Canonical formula: $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$.
- **Scale factor** $\sqrt{d_k}$ is essential because $\text{Var}(q^{\top} k) = d_k$.
- **Self-Attention:** Q, K, V all derived from the same input.
- **Cross-Attention:** Q from one sequence, K and V from another.
- **Multi-Head Attention:** $h$ parallel attention operations over projected subspaces, concatenated and linearly combined.
- **Causal Masking:** Sets upper-triangular entries to $-\infty$ before softmax for autoregressive models (e.g., GPT).
- **Complexity:** $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ — quadratic in sequence length is the bottleneck for long-context models.
- **Variance Result:** Always remember $\text{Var}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k}) = 1$ — frequently tested.
- **Bahdanau vs Luong:** Additive uses a feed-forward alignment net; Luong uses dot product — Transformer uses Scaled Dot-Product.
- **Softmax rows** of the attention matrix sum to **1** — useful sanity check in numerical problems.
- **Engineering Applications:** Translation, summarization, vision (ViT), protein folding (AlphaFold), speech (Whisper).
- **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ factor is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT756 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications in Various Fields: Art and Creativity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generative AI in Art and Creativity** refers to the application of generative models—such as **Large Language Models (LLMs)**, **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**, **Diffusion Models**, and **Transformer-based multimodal systems**—to autonomously or semi-autonomously produce, augment, or transform creative artifacts including visual art, music, literature, film, design, fashion, architecture, and interactive media.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, this domain sits at the intersection of **computational creativity**, **machine learning**, and **human-computer interaction (HCI)**. The output of such systems is judged not merely on statistical likelihood but on aesthetic, semantic, and emotional qualities evaluated through human perception studies and established creative benchmarks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Infinite Art Studio

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine an apprentice artist who has spent their entire life in a vast museum, observing every painting, sculpture, melody, and poem ever recorded. When you whisper a description—"a cyberpunk temple at sunset in the style of Van Gogh"—this apprentice does not *copy* a single painting. Instead, they reach into their internal library of patterns, blend textures, compositions, and color theories learned from millions of examples, and paint something *new*. That apprentice is your **Generative AI model**.

The key insight is that the model does not memorize—it **generalizes the statistical structure** of creative works and synthesizes novel samples from the learned distribution. The phrase used in KTU textbooks is **"learning the data distribution $p(x)$ and sampling from it."**

## 1.3 Core Modalities of Generative Creative Output

| Modality | Representative Models | Output Type |
|---|---|---|
| Text-to-Image | DALL-E 2/3, Stable Diffusion, Midjourney, Imagen | Static & high-resolution images |
| Text-to-Video | Sora, Runway Gen-2/3, Pika | Short cinematic clips |
| Text-to-Audio/Music | MusicLM, Jukebox, AIVA, Suno AI | Songs, ambient soundtracks, voice |
| Text-to-3D | DreamFusion, Point-E, Magic3D | Meshes, NeRFs, point clouds |
| Text-to-Code & Literature | GPT-4, Claude, Copilot | Poetry, screenplays, software |
| Style Transfer | Neural Style Transfer (Gatys et al.), AdaIN | Reimagined artworks |

## 1.4 The Three Foundational Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every creative generative AI application rests on three pillars that KTU examiners frequently test:
>
> 1. **Latent Space Learning** — Compressing the creative domain into a continuous mathematical manifold $\mathcal{Z}$ where similar concepts cluster together.
> 2. **Conditional Generation** — Steering the sampling process via text prompts, class labels, sketches, or audio cues using cross-attention or classifier-free guidance.
> 3. **Perceptual Quality Optimization** — Training objectives that align the output with human aesthetic judgments (e.g., CLIP-based reward models, human preference fine-tuning via RLHF).

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters for KTU 2024

The 2024 scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. For this module, the relevant Course Outcomes (COs) are:

- **CO4 (Module 4):** *Illustrate the diverse applications of generative models across domains including art, science, healthcare, and business.*

This topic directly maps to the higher Bloom's levels: **Apply (L3)**, **Analyze (L4)**, and **Evaluate (L5)**—which is why exam questions typically require students to *compare models*, *critique outputs*, and *propose pipelines* rather than simply recall definitions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How Generative Models "Create"

The act of creation, mathematically, is the process of drawing samples from a learned probability distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$. The model approximates this with $p_{\theta}(x)$ and samples from it.

### 2.1.1 The Latent Variable Formulation

Most modern creative generators (VAEs, Diffusion Models, Latent Diffusion Models) operate in a **latent space** $\mathcal{Z} \subset \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d \ll D$ (the original data dimensionality). The generation pipeline is:

1. **Encode:** A real artwork $x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)$ is mapped to a latent vector $z = E(x) \in \mathcal{Z}$.
2. **Sample:** A new latent $z' \sim p(z)$ is drawn (often Gaussian or learned).
3. **Decode:** A new artwork $\tilde{x} = D(z')$ is synthesized.

The model is trained so that $\tilde{x}$ looks indistinguishable from real $x$.

### 2.1.2 The Diffusion Process (used by Stable Diffusion, Imagen, DALL-E 3)

The forward process gradually adds Gaussian noise over $T$ timesteps:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

The reverse process learns to denoise step-by-step, predicting the noise $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ that was added.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Diffusion is like watching a clear photograph slowly dissolve into static TV snow (forward), then training a neural network to *reverse* the process and recover a brand-new coherent image from pure noise (reverse). The text prompt is injected at every denoising step via **cross-attention**.

### 2.1.3 The GAN Framework (used by StyleGAN, BigGAN)

Two networks play a minimax game:

$$\min_{G}\max_{D}\, V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

- **Generator $G$:** Creates fake artworks.
- **Discriminator $D$:** Tries to distinguish real from fake.

Through adversarial training, $G$ learns to fool $D$, producing samples that match the artistic distribution.

### 2.1.4 CLIP Guidance — Bridging Text and Vision

**CLIP** (Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training) learns a joint embedding where matching (image, text) pairs are close. The text-to-image generator is trained to maximize:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CLIP}} = -\log \frac{\exp(\cos(E_I(x), E_T(c))/\tau)}{\sum_{c'} \exp(\cos(E_I(x), E_T(c'))/\tau)}$$

where $E_I$ and $E_T$ are the image and text encoders, $c$ is the caption, and $\tau$ is a temperature parameter.

## 2.2 Domain-Specific Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Visual Art & Image Synthesis

**Pipeline Architecture (Latent Diffusion):**

$$\text{Text Prompt} \xrightarrow{\text{CLIP Text Encoder}} \text{Text Embedding} \xrightarrow{\text{Cross-Attention}} \text{UNet Denoiser} \rightarrow \text{Latent} \xrightarrow{\text{VAE Decoder}} \text{Image}$$

Key innovations evaluated in KTU exams:
- **Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG):** Trades diversity for prompt adherence. A higher CFG scale $s$ sharpens outputs but reduces variation:
$$\hat{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, c) = (1 + w)\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w\, \epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$$
where $w$ is the guidance weight.

- **ControlNet:** Adds spatial conditioning (edges, depth, pose) to lock down the generative freedom.

### 2.2.2 Music & Audio Generation

**MusicLM (Google, 2023)** uses a hierarchical sequence-to-sequence model:
- **Stage 1:** MuLan embeddings convert text → semantic audio tokens.
- **Stage 2:** SoundStream decodes tokens → 24 kHz audio waveforms.

The autoregressive token prediction is:

$$p(a_t \mid a_{<t}, c) = \text{softmax}(W_o \cdot h_t)$$

where $h_t$ is the hidden state from a Transformer and $c$ is the conditioning text embedding.

### 2.2.3 Literature & Creative Writing

LLMs use **autoregressive next-token prediction**:

$$p(x_{1:T}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p(x_t \mid x_{<t})$$

For creative writing, **temperature sampling** and **top-$p$ (nucleus) sampling** control diversity:

$$p'(x_t \mid x_{<t}) = \frac{p(x_t \mid x_{<t})^{1/T}}{\sum_{x} p(x \mid x_{<t})^{1/T}}$$

A **temperature** $T > 1$ increases randomness (more "creative"), while $T < 1$ sharpens the distribution.

### 2.2.4 Video Generation

Models like **Sora** (OpenAI) treat video as a sequence of spacetime patches ("visual tokens") processed by a Diffusion Transformer (DiT). The generation is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Sora}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c_{\text{text}}) \Vert^2\right]$$

where $x_0$ is the raw video, $c_{\text{text}}$ is the text prompt, and the loss is the standard diffusion MSE between true and predicted noise.

### 2.2.5 Style Transfer (The Classic Gatys Method)

Given a content image $x_c$ and style image $x_s$, the loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \alpha\, \mathcal{L}_{\text{content}}(x, x_c) + \beta\, \mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}(x, x_s)$$

Content loss uses high-level feature maps:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{content}} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} (F_{ij}^l - P_{ij}^l)^2$$

Style loss uses **Gram matrices** $G^l = F^l (F^l)^T$ to capture texture statistics:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{style}} = \sum_l w_l \cdot \frac{1}{4 N_l^2 M_l^2} \sum_{i,j} (G_{ij}^l - A_{ij}^l)^2$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Art & Creativity Applications** — Master these for Part B answers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diffusion Forward | $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon$ | $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}\alpha_s$ |
| 2 | Diffusion Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{t,x_0,\epsilon}[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t) \Vert^2]$ | $\epsilon_\theta$ = noise predictor |
| 3 | GAN Objective | $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$ | $G$, $D$ nets |
| 4 | VAE ELBO | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q(z\mid x)}[\log p(x\mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q(z\mid x) \Vert p(z))$ | ELBO |
| 5 | CFG | $\hat{\epsilon} = (1+w)\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$ | $w$ = guidance weight |
| 6 | CLIP Loss | $-\log \frac{\exp(\cos(E_I,E_T)/\tau)}{\sum_{c'}\exp(\cos(E_I,E_{T'})/\tau)}$ | $\tau$ = temperature |
| 7 | Gram Matrix | $G^l_{ij} = \sum_k F^l_{ik} F^l_{jk}$ | $F^l$ = feature map |
| 8 | Autoregressive | $p(x_{1:T}) = \prod_t p(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | Token sampling |
| 9 | Temperature | $p'(x) \propto p(x)^{1/T}$ | $T > 1$ = more random |
| 10 | Top-$p$ | Sort probs, take smallest set with cumulative $\geq p$ | Nucleus sampling |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation: The Diffusion Forward Process Closed Form

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **exam-favorite** because it directly links the noise schedule to model training and is non-trivial enough to score 7 marks.

### Step 1 — Define the Recurrence

The forward diffusion at each small step adds a small amount of Gaussian noise:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t}\, x_{t-1} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}, \quad \epsilon_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

### Step 2 — Expand Two Steps

Substitute $x_{t-1}$ into $x_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= \sqrt{\alpha_t}\bigl(\sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}}\, \epsilon_{t-2}\bigr) + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1} \\
&= \sqrt{\alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{\alpha_t(1-\alpha_{t-1})}\, \epsilon_{t-2} + \sqrt{1-\alpha_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine Two Gaussians

Since the sum of two independent Gaussians is Gaussian, and the coefficient of $x_{t-2}$ is $\sqrt{\alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}}$, the variance must be:

$$\text{Variance} = \alpha_t(1-\alpha_{t-1}) + (1-\alpha_t) = 1 - \alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}$$

So:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}}\, \tilde{\epsilon}$$

where $\tilde{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ (the two $\epsilon$'s merge into a single Gaussian).

### Step 4 — Induct to All $t$ Steps

By induction, the closed-form expression becomes:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon$$

where $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s$.

### Step 5 — Interpretation

As $t \to T$, $\bar{\alpha}_T \to 0$, so $x_T \approx \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — pure noise. **This proves that diffusion gradually destroys signal and converges to the standard normal prior**, which is what enables sampling from noise at inference time.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Creative Generative Model Families

| Criterion | GANs | VAEs | Diffusion | Autoregressive LMs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output quality (FID)** | Excellent on faces (StyleGAN3), mode collapse risk | Blurry | State-of-the-art (Imagen, DALL-E 3) | High for text/audio |
| **Training stability** | Unstable (minimax) | Stable | Very stable | Stable |
| **Sampling speed** | Fast (single forward) | Fast | Slow (50–1000 steps) | Slow for long sequences |
| **Mode coverage** | Low (mode collapse) | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Best for** | High-res faces, video game art | Latent interpolation | Photorealism, art, video | Text, music, code |
| **Representative** | StyleGAN3, BigGAN | NVAE, VQ-VAE | Stable Diffusion, Imagen | GPT-4, MusicLM |
| **Examiner signal** | Older but tested | Foundational | Most modern KTU questions | Always appears |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Evaluating a Creative Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A startup wants to generate artwork for a children's book. They have 500 style reference images. Build them a pipeline.

**Step 1 — Choose model family.** Since they need artistic style fidelity and a small dataset (500 images), fine-tuning a **Stable Diffusion** model with **LoRA** (Low-Rank Adaptation) is ideal.

**Step 2 — Apply LoRA.** LoRA freezes the original UNet weights $W_0$ and adds a low-rank update $\Delta W = BA$:

$$W' = W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} BA$$

where $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}$, and $r \ll \min(d, k)$.

**Step 3 — Train.** Update only $A$ and $B$ on the 500 reference images. Compute the standard diffusion MSE loss (Equation from §2.1.2):

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c_{\text{prompt}}) \Vert^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Sample.** At inference, run the reverse diffusion with a CFG scale $w \approx 7.5$ and the children's book prompt $c_{\text{prompt}}$.

**Step 5 — Quality check.** Use **CLIP score** $\text{CLIP}(x, c)$ as an automated proxy for prompt alignment, and human raters for aesthetic evaluation.
