# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Explain in brief how CLIP (Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training) enables text-to-image generation. State its key limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

CLIP is trained on 400 million (image, text) pairs using contrastive learning. It learns a joint embedding space in which semantically related images and text captions have similar vector representations.

$$\text{score}(x, c) = \cos\bigl(E_I(x),\, E_T(c)\bigr)$$

During text-to-image generation, the diffusion model uses CLIP's text encoder to embed the prompt. At each denoising step, cross-attention conditions the image generation on this embedding, pulling the visual output toward the semantic content of the prompt.

**Key Limitation:** CLIP struggles with compositional reasoning (e.g., "a red book on top of a blue pen") and may produce outputs that match *some* concepts in the prompt but not the *spatial relationships*. It also exhibits social and cultural biases inherited from its web-scraped training data.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for embedding concept, 1 mark for cross-attention role, 1 mark for limitation.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Differentiate between Classifier Guidance and Classifier-Free Guidance in diffusion models. Which is preferred in modern text-to-image systems?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Classifier Guidance | Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) |
|---|---|---|
| Extra model needed | Yes — a separately trained classifier $p_\phi(y \mid x_t)$ | No — uses the same diffusion model with empty prompt $\emptyset$ |
| Gradient term | $\nabla_{x_t} \log p_\phi(y \mid x_t)$ added to noise prediction | Linear interpolation between conditional and unconditional predictions |
| Computational cost | Higher (extra forward pass) | Lower (single forward pass with dropout-style conditioning) |
| Flexibility | Limited to trained classes | Works with any text prompt |

**CFG Formula:**

$$\hat{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, c) = (1 + w)\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w\, \epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$$

**Preferred:** Modern systems (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3, Imagen) use **Classifier-Free Guidance** because it avoids training a separate classifier, supports arbitrary text conditioning, and produces sharper prompt-aligned outputs at higher guidance weights $w$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition each, 1 mark preference with justification.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the architecture of a **Latent Diffusion Model (LDM)** as used in Stable Diffusion for text-to-image generation. Include the role of the VAE encoder/decoder, the UNet denoiser, and the CLIP text encoder in your explanation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of the diffusion forward equation, explain how an image is gradually corrupted into noise and then reconstructed. Show how the closed-form expression $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon$ is derived.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
The Latent Diffusion Model operates in three stages: (i) encoding an image into a lower-dimensional latent space, (ii) performing the diffusion process on the latent representation conditioned on a text prompt, and (iii) decoding the denoised latent back into pixel space.

**Stage 1 — VAE Encoder (1.5 marks):**
A pretrained Variational Autoencoder encoder $E$ maps an image $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ into a compact latent $z = E(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times w \times c}$, where $h = H/8$, $w = W/8$. This reduces computational cost by a factor of $64\times$ compared to pixel-space diffusion. The VAE is trained with a perceptual loss plus a KL-divergence regularization toward a standard normal prior.

**Stage 2 — UNet Denoiser with Cross-Attention (2 marks):**
The UNet $\epsilon_\theta$ takes the noisy latent $z_t$ at timestep $t$ and predicts the noise $\epsilon$ added. It receives text conditioning through **cross-attention layers**:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

Here $Q = W_Q z_t$, $K = W_K c$, $V = W_V c$, where $c$ is the text embedding from CLIP. This injects semantic meaning at every denoising step.

**Stage 3 — VAE Decoder (1.5 marks):**
After $T$ denoising iterations, the refined latent $z_0$ is passed through the VAE decoder $D$ to reconstruct the final high-resolution image $\tilde{x} = D(z_0)$.

**Summary diagram in words:** Text → CLIP → embedding → cross-attention into UNet → iterate $T$ steps → clean latent → VAE decoder → image.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Forward Process Definition (1.5 marks):**
At each timestep $t \in \{1, 2, \dots, T\}$, a small amount of Gaussian noise is added:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t}\, x_{t-1} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}, \quad \epsilon_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

where $\alpha_t \in (0, 1)$ follows a variance schedule (linear, cosine, etc.).

**Two-Step Expansion (2 marks):**
Substitute recursively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= \sqrt{\alpha_t}\bigl(\sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t-1}}\, \epsilon_{t-2}\bigr) + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1} \\
&= \sqrt{\alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{\alpha_t(1-\alpha_{t-1})}\, \epsilon_{t-2} + \sqrt{1-\alpha_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combine Gaussian Terms (1.5 marks):**
Sum of two independent zero-mean Gaussians with variances $\sigma_1^2$ and $\sigma_2^2$ yields a Gaussian with variance $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2$:

$$\alpha_t(1-\alpha_{t-1}) + (1-\alpha_t) = 1 - \alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}$$

So:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}}\, x_{t-2} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t \alpha_{t-1}}\, \tilde{\epsilon}$$

**Inductive Generalization (2 marks):**
By induction over $t$ steps:

$$x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s$$

This closed form lets us **directly sample $x_t$ for any $t$ without iterating** — a critical engineering optimization that makes diffusion training tractable. As $t \to T$, $\bar{\alpha}_T \approx 0$, and $x_T \approx \epsilon$ — i.e., the image becomes indistinguishable from pure Gaussian noise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Repeated Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the application of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) in art creation. With the GAN objective function $\min_G \max_D V(D, G)$ as reference, explain how StyleGAN achieves high-fidelity face generation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Neural Style Transfer** using the Gatys et al. formulation. State the content loss, style loss (with Gram matrices), and the total loss. How is the trade-off parameter $\alpha / \beta$ used?

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**GANs in Art (2 marks):**
GANs have been pivotal in creative AI since 2014. They are used for face synthesis (StyleGAN, StyleGAN2, StyleGAN3), anime character generation, sketch-to-image translation (Pix2Pix), and style-aware image translation (CycleGAN for turning horses into zebras, photos into Monet paintings). The adversarial setup forces the generator to produce samples statistically indistinguishable from real artworks.

**GAN Objective (2 marks):**
The two-player minimax game:

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

- $D$ maximizes to correctly classify real ($x$) vs fake ($G(z)$).
- $G$ minimizes to fool $D$.

At Nash equilibrium, $p_G = p_{\text{data}}$ and $D(x) = 1/2$ everywhere.

**StyleGAN's Innovations (3 marks):**
1. **Mapping Network:** A 8-layer MLP maps the latent $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ to an intermediate latent $w \in \mathcal{W}$, which **disentangles** semantic attributes (pose, lighting, identity, style).
2. **Adaptive Instance Normalization (AdaIN):** At each generator layer, $w$ modulates the style:

$$\text{AdaIN}(x_i, y) = \sigma(y)\left(\frac{x_i - \mu(x_i)}{\sigma(x_i)}\right) + \mu(y)$$

3. **Progressive Growing:** Resolution is increased from $4 \times 4$ up to $1024 \times 1024$, stabilizing training and improving fine-detail realism.
4. **Style Mixing:** Different $w$ vectors feed different layers, enabling fine-grained control over coarse vs fine features — a creative artist's tool.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Gatys Style Transfer Setup (1.5 marks):**
Given a content image $x_c$, a style image $x_s$, and a randomly initialized white-noise image $x$, the algorithm iteratively updates $x$ to minimize a weighted sum of content and style losses using backpropagation through a pretrained VGG-19 network.

**Content Loss (2 marks):**
Using feature maps at layer $l$ (typically `conv4_2`):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{content}}(x, x_c) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \bigl(F_{ij}^l(x) - P_{ij}^l(x_c)\bigr)^2$$

**Style Loss Using Gram Matrices (2.5 marks):**
The Gram matrix $G^l \in \mathbb{R}^{N_l \times N_l}$ captures correlations between feature channels and thus encodes *texture*:

$$G_{ij}^l(x) = \sum_k F_{ik}^l(x)\, F_{jk}^l(x)$$

Style loss at layer $l$ is the squared Frobenius distance between Gram matrices of $x$ and $x_s$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}^l(x, x_s) = \frac{1}{4 N_l^2 M_l^2} \sum_{i,j} \bigl(G_{ij}^l(x) - A_{ij}^l(x_s)\bigr)^2$$

Total style loss aggregates over multiple layers (e.g., `conv1_1`, `conv2_1`, `conv3_1`, `conv4_1`, `conv5_1`):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}(x, x_s) = \sum_l w_l\, \mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}^l(x, x_s)$$

**Total Loss and Trade-off (1 mark):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}(x, x_c, x_s) = \alpha\, \mathcal{L}_{\text{content}}(x, x_c) + \beta\, \mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}(x, x_s)$$

- **Large $\alpha / \beta$:** Preserves content structure; light stylization.
- **Small $\alpha / \beta$:** Heavy stylization; content may be warped.

The artist tunes this ratio to balance recognizable subject matter with the desired artistic flourish.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students most commonly **lose marks** on this topic in the following ways:
>
> 1. **Confusing VAE vs Diffusion:** VAEs use an explicit encoder–decoder bottleneck with a KL term. Diffusion models have **no encoder** for the forward process — only a noise scheduler. Mentioning "VAE-like encoder in diffusion" loses you a mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\bar{\alpha}_t$ definition:** Many write $x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t} x_0 + \dots$ instead of $\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} x_0$. The product over timesteps is essential.
> 3. **Skipping the induction step:** Derivation questions require explicit expansion of $x_t \to x_{t-2} \to \dots \to x_0$, not just stating the closed form.
> 4. **Mixing up Gram matrices:** Students often confuse the Gram matrix with the feature map itself. State clearly: $G = F F^\top$ is a *correlation* matrix, not the features.
> 5. **No real-world link:** For 14-mark answers, always conclude with a domain application (e.g., "this is used in Midjourney's v6 release") to demonstrate depth per OBE.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎨 **Generative AI in art** is the use of learned probability models to *sample* novel creative artifacts from the distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$.
- 🖼️ **Text-to-image pipelines** = **Text Encoder (CLIP/T5)** + **Latent Diffusion UNet** + **VAE Decoder**.
- 🎵 **MusicLM** and **Jukebox** are the canonical examples for audio, using hierarchical token prediction.
- 📐 **Style transfer** = $\mathcal{L} = \alpha \mathcal{L}_{\text{content}} + \beta \mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}$ with Gram matrices for style.
- ⚔️ **GAN objective:** $\min_G \max_D$ game; generator and discriminator train adversarially.
- 🌫️ **Diffusion closed form:** $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon$ with $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_s \alpha_s$.
- 🎛️ **Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG)** uses weight $w$ to sharpen outputs toward the prompt.
- 🎮 **StyleGAN** innovations: mapping network $z \to w$, AdaIN modulation, progressive growing.
- ✍️ **Autoregressive LLMs** use $p(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ with temperature $T$ and top-$p$ sampling for creative text.
- 🎬 **Sora and video DiTs** extend diffusion to spacetime patches for video.
- ⚖️ **Ethical concerns:** copyright (training data), deepfakes, bias, attribution, and labor displacement.
- 🧠 **Remember the three pillars:** latent space + conditional generation + perceptual optimization.
- 📊 **KTU mark signal:** Always cite the *model family* (GAN/VAE/Diffusion/AR), *loss function*, and *real-world example* for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT756 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Text Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Text Generation** is the task in Natural Language Generation (NLG) where a generative model produces coherent, contextually relevant, and semantically meaningful sequences of natural language tokens (words, sub-words, or characters) conditioned on an input prompt, context vector, or learned latent representation. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Generative AI, text generation refers specifically to the autoregressive and transformer-based procedures by which large language models (LLMs) like **GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer)** sample one token at a time from a learned probability distribution over the vocabulary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Text Generation is the process of producing natural language output by modeling the conditional probability distribution $P(y_t \vert y_{<t}, x)$, where $y_t$ is the next token to be generated, $y_{<t}$ represents all previously generated tokens, and $x$ is the optional input context.

Mathematically, the joint probability of generating a sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T)$ is factorized using the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(\mathbf{y}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_t \mid y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{t-1})$$

Each factor $P(y_t \mid y_{<t})$ is computed by passing the token embeddings through a stack of transformer decoder blocks and applying a **softmax** over the vocabulary dimension $V$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Auto-complete Storyteller"

Imagine you are a student sitting in a classroom. The teacher begins narrating a story: *"Once upon a time, in a small village nestled in the Western Ghats of Kerala…"* — and then pauses. Your job is to say the **next single word**. You could say "a", "the", "there", "lived", or "boy". Each choice is plausible, but only one (or a small set) keeps the story coherent.

Text generation works exactly the same way — except the "student" is a transformer with **billions of parameters**, and instead of predicting one word, it does so **autoregressively** (i.e., it appends each new word to the running sentence and re-reads the whole sequence before predicting the next). This loop continues until a special **end-of-sequence (EOS)** token is emitted, which acts like a "full stop" signal telling the model to stop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Text generation is NOT a one-shot transformation. It is an **iterative sampling process**. Each token is drawn, then fed back into the model to influence the prediction of the next token. This is why generated text can "drift" or "hallucinate" — early mistakes compound downstream.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Token** | The smallest discrete unit a model operates on (word, sub-word, or character). |
| **Vocabulary ($V$)** | The complete set of unique tokens the model recognizes (often 32k–100k+). |
| **Autoregressive** | Generating output one step at a time, where each step depends on prior steps. |
| **Softmax Temperature ($\tau$)** | A scalar that controls the "sharpness" of the output probability distribution. |
| **Perplexity (PPL)** | A standard metric: lower PPL means the model is more confident. PPL = $\exp(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log P)$ |
| **BLEU / ROUGE** | N-gram overlap metrics between generated and reference text (translation, summarization). |
| **EOS Token** | Special symbol (e.g., `<|endoftext|>`) marking sequence termination. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants/Defaults:** Vocabulary size for GPT-2 ≈ **50,257 tokens**; context length for GPT-3 = **2048 tokens**; typical default sampling temperature $\tau = \mathbf{1.0}$; top-p nucleus threshold commonly set to **0.9**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Autoregressive Generation Pipeline

Text generation in modern LLMs follows a strict five-stage pipeline at inference time:

1. **Tokenization:** The input prompt is split into integer token IDs using a tokenizer (e.g., **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** or **SentencePiece**).
2. **Embedding Lookup:** Each token ID is mapped to a dense vector of dimension $d_{\text{model}}$ (e.g., $d_{\text{model}} = 768$ for GPT-2 small, $4096$ for GPT-3 175B).
3. **Transformer Forward Pass:** The embedded tokens pass through $L$ decoder blocks (each with masked multi-head self-attention and a feed-forward network). The final hidden state for position $t$ is $\mathbf{h}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}}}$.
4. **LM Head Projection:** $\mathbf{h}_t$ is projected onto the vocabulary space: $\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{h}_t \mathbf{W}_{\text{lm}}$ where $\mathbf{W}_{\text{lm}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times \vert V \vert}$.
5. **Sampling/Decoding:** The logits $\mathbf{z}_t$ are converted to a probability distribution (often via temperature-scaled softmax), and the next token is **sampled** or **selected**.

---

## 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why tokenize?** Neural networks operate on numerical vectors, not raw strings. Sub-word tokenization balances vocabulary size with the ability to handle rare/out-of-vocabulary words.
- **Why masked self-attention?** During training and generation, position $t$ must not "see" future positions (tokens at $t+1, t+2, \ldots$). The causal mask $\mathbf{M}$ is an upper-triangular matrix of $-\infty$ that zeroes out future attention weights after softmax.
- **Why a separate LM head?** Decoupling the body of the transformer from the vocabulary projection allows weight tying and easier fine-tuning.

---

## 2.3 Decoding Strategies — The Core Variants

### A. Greedy Decoding
At each timestep, pick the token with the **highest** probability.

$$y_t^{\text{greedy}} = \arg\max_{v \in V} \; P(v \mid y_{<t})$$

- **Pros:** Fast, deterministic, simple.
- **Cons:** Prone to repetition loops and generic outputs ("The the the…").

### B. Beam Search
Maintain the top-$k$ (beam width) highest-probability partial sequences at each step. After $T$ steps, return the sequence with the highest cumulative log-probability.

$$\text{score}(\mathbf{y}) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t})$$

- **Pros:** Higher BLEU scores in translation tasks.
- **Cons:** Slower by factor $k$; can still produce repetitive text in open-ended generation.

### C. Sampling with Temperature
Instead of argmax, sample from a temperature-scaled softmax:

$$P_{\tau}(y_t = v \mid y_{<t}) = \frac{\exp(z_{t,v}/\tau)}{\sum_{v' \in V} \exp(z_{t,v'}/\tau)}$$

- $\tau \to 0$: distribution becomes a one-hot (greedy).
- $\tau = 1$: original distribution.
- $\tau > 1$: distribution flattens → more random/creative output.

### D. Top-$k$ Sampling
Restrict sampling to the **top $k$ most probable** tokens, then redistribute probability mass among them.

### E. Top-$p$ (Nucleus) Sampling
Sort tokens by descending probability. Find the smallest set $S$ such that $\sum_{v \in S} P(v \mid y_{<t}) \geq p$. Sample only from $S$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Note:** Modern LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) typically expose `temperature`, `top_p`, and `top_k` as user-controllable parameters. The default recommendation is $\tau = 0.7$, $p = 0.9$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chain rule (sequence) | $P(\mathbf{y}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_t \mid y_{<t})$ | Foundation of autoregressive generation |
| 2 | Softmax (with temperature) | $P(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v/\tau)}{\sum_{v'} \exp(z_{v'}/\tau)}$ | $\tau > 0$; default $\tau=1$ |
| 3 | Greedy decode | $y_t^* = \arg\max_v P(v \mid y_{<t})$ | Deterministic; fast |
| 4 | Beam score | $\text{score}(\mathbf{y}) = \sum_t \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t})$ | Often length-normalized: $\vert\mathbf{y}\vert^{\alpha}$ |
| 5 | Perplexity | $\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P(y_i)\right)$ | Lower is better; eval-time metric |
| 6 | Top-$p$ (nucleus) | $\sum_{v \in S_p} P(v) \geq p$, $S_p$ minimal | Typical $p = 0.9$ |
| 7 | BLEU (simplified) | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $p_n$ = modified n-gram precision; BP = brevity penalty |
| 8 | Attention (scaled dot-prod) | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $d_k$ = key dim; critical for long contexts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These decoding strategies directly affect deployed LLM behavior in customer-service chatbots, code generation (Copilot), translation (Google Translate), and creative writing tools. Choosing the right decoding strategy is a **production engineering** decision, not just an academic one.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Logits to Temperature-Scaled Softmax

**Starting Point.** A transformer produces a logit vector $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_{\vert V \vert})$ at time $t$, where $z_v$ is the unnormalized score for token $v$.

**Step 1 — Apply temperature scaling.** Divide each logit by $\tau$:

$$z_v' = \frac{z_v}{\tau}$$

This stretches (when $\tau < 1$) or compresses (when $\tau > 1$) the relative differences between logits.

**Step 2 — Exponentiate.** Convert scaled logits to non-negative values:

$$u_v = \exp(z_v') = \exp(z_v / \tau)$$

**Step 3 — Normalize.** Divide by the sum of all exponentiated logits to obtain a valid probability distribution:

$$P_{\tau}(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify constraints.** (a) $P_{\tau}(v) > 0$ for all $v$ (since exponential is always positive). (b) $\sum_v P_{\tau}(v) = 1$ (by construction of the denominator).

**Step 5 — Limiting cases.**

$$\lim_{\tau \to 0^+} P_{\tau}(v) = \begin{cases} 1 & v = \arg\max_j z_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This recovers greedy decoding. At $\tau = 1$, the standard softmax is recovered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Temperature acts like a "creativity dial." At low $\tau$, the model becomes overconfident and deterministic; at high $\tau$, it becomes indecisive and may produce nonsense. The sweet spot for most applications lies between $\tau = 0.7$ and $\tau = 1.0$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Top-$p$ (Nucleus) Sampling on a 5-Token Vocabulary

Suppose at some timestep the unscaled logits (already softmaxed for simplicity) give:

| Token | Probability |
|---|---|
| "cat" | 0.50 |
| "dog" | 0.25 |
| "bird" | 0.15 |
| "fish" | 0.07 |
| "snake" | 0.03 |

**Set $p = 0.9$.** Compute cumulative sums in descending order:

1. "cat": cumulative = 0.50 → not yet $\geq 0.9$
2. "cat" + "dog": cumulative = 0.75 → still not $\geq 0.9$
3. "cat" + "dog" + "bird": cumulative = **0.90** → **STOP**. Nucleus $S_p = \{\text{cat, dog, bird}\}$.

**Renormalize** within $S_p$:

$$P'(\text{cat}) = \frac{0.50}{0.90} \approx 0.556, \quad P'(\text{dog}) = \frac{0.25}{0.90} \approx 0.278, \quad P'(\text{bird}) = \frac{0.15}{0.90} \approx 0.167$$

Sample one token according to this renormalized distribution. Tokens "fish" and "snake" are entirely excluded from this step, even though they had non-zero original probability. This prevents the model from accidentally picking a low-probability tail token.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Perplexity Calculation

Given a test set with $N = 3$ tokens, with model log-probabilities:

$$\log P(y_1) = -2.0, \quad \log P(y_2) = -0.5, \quad \log P(y_3) = -3.0$$

**Step 1 — Compute the average negative log-likelihood:**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(y_i) = -\frac{1}{3}\big(-2.0 + (-0.5) + (-3.0)\big) = -\frac{1}{3}(-5.5) = 1.833$$

**Step 2 — Exponentiate to get perplexity:**

$$\text{PPL} = \exp(\mathcal{L}) = \exp(1.833) \approx 6.25$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the model is "as unsure as choosing uniformly among 6.25 tokens." Lower is better.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decoding Strategy Comparison

| Strategy | Determinism | Diversity | Best Use-Case | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Greedy** | Fully deterministic | Very low | Factual QA, code completion | Repetition loops |
| **Beam ($k=5$)** | Deterministic (with seed) | Low–medium | Machine translation, summarization | Generic phrasing |
| **Sampling ($\tau=1$)** | Stochastic | High | Creative writing, brainstorming | Incoherence, hallucinations |
| **Top-$k$ ($k=40$)** | Stochastic | Medium–high | Dialogue, story generation | Can still pick bad tokens if $k$ too large |
| **Top-$p$ ($p=0.9$)** | Stochastic | Adaptive | General-purpose chatbots | Threshold-dependent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Autoregressive Generation Loop (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Prompt: Token IDs x1 x2 ... xn] --> B[Token Embedding + Positional Encoding]
    B --> C[Transformer Decoder Stack: L layers]
    C --> D[LM Head: Project to vocab logits z]
    D --> E{Decoding Strategy?}
    E -->|Greedy| F1[Argmax over vocab]
    E -->|Beam k| F2[Keep top-k beams]
    E -->|Top-p| F3[Nucleus sampling p=0.9]
    E -->|Temp tau| F4[Softmax with temperature tau]
    F1 --> G[Sample / Select next token yt]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H{EOS token?}
    H -->|No| I[Append yt to sequence y< t = y1... yt]
    I --> C
    H -->|Yes| J[Final Output Sequence]
```

---

## 4.2 Transformer Decoder Block (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Input Embeddings] --> MHA[Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    MHA --> AddNorm1[Add and LayerNorm]
    AddNorm1 --> FFN[Feed-Forward Network: 4dmodel hidden]
    FFN --> AddNorm2[Add and LayerNorm]
    AddNorm2 --> Out[Output to next block or LM Head]
```

---

## 4.3 Decoding Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to pick next token] --> Q1{Is task open-ended or constrained?}
    Q1 -->|Constrained: QA, code| Q2{Need exact answer?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Greedy[Use Greedy Decoding]
    Q2 -->|No| Beam[Use Beam Search k=4]
    Q1 -->|Open-ended: chat, story| Q3{Need diversity?}
    Q3 -->|Low diversity| TopK[Top-k, k=40, tau=0.7]
    Q3 -->|High diversity| TopP[Top-p=0.9, tau=0.9]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a functional module in the inference pipeline. The recursive arrow from `G → I → C` captures the **autoregressive loop** — the defining characteristic of how modern LLMs generate text one token at a time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** 
**Define text generation in the context of Generative AI. Mention any two decoding strategies used in autoregressive language models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Text generation is the task of producing natural language sequences by modeling the conditional probability $P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$ over a vocabulary $V$, where each token is sampled autoregressively conditioned on previously generated tokens and the input context. **(1.5 marks)**

Two commonly used decoding strategies are:
1. **Greedy Decoding** — selects $\arg\max_v P(v \mid y_{<t})$ at each step. **(0.75 marks)**
2. **Top-$p$ (Nucleus) Sampling** — samples from the smallest token set whose cumulative probability exceeds threshold $p$. **(0.75 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**
**Explain the role of the temperature parameter $\tau$ in text generation. What happens when $\tau \to 0$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The temperature parameter $\tau$ scales the logits before the softmax operation, controlling the **sharpness** of the output probability distribution. **(1 mark)**

The temperature-scaled softmax is: $P_\tau(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v/\tau)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j/\tau)}$ **(1 mark)**

When $\tau \to 0$, the distribution collapses to a one-hot vector over the highest-logit token, effectively recovering **greedy decoding**. The output becomes fully deterministic. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the autoregressive text generation pipeline in a transformer-based language model. Discuss the role of the LM head and the causal mask. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast Greedy decoding, Beam Search, and Top-$p$ sampling. Under what conditions would you prefer each? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pipeline Overview (3 marks):**
The autoregressive pipeline consists of: (i) **Tokenization** of input text into sub-word IDs, (ii) **Embedding lookup** combining token and positional embeddings, (iii) **Transformer decoder forward pass** through $L$ layers of masked self-attention and feed-forward networks, (iv) **LM head projection** $\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{h}_t \mathbf{W}_{\text{lm}}$ producing logits over vocabulary $V$, (v) **Decoding/sampling** to produce the next token $y_t$, which is then fed back into the input sequence.

**Step 2 — Role of LM Head (2 marks):**
The LM head is a learnable linear layer of shape $\mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times \vert V \vert}$ that maps the final hidden representation $\mathbf{h}_t$ to unnormalized scores (logits) over every possible token in the vocabulary. Without it, the model would produce abstract representations rather than token probabilities.

**Step 3 — Role of Causal Mask (2 marks):**
The causal (look-ahead) mask $\mathbf{M}$ is an upper-triangular matrix of $-\infty$ added to attention scores before softmax. This ensures that position $t$ can only attend to positions $1, \ldots, t$ — never to future positions. It is essential because during training the model sees the entire sequence at once, but during inference we must prevent "cheating" by peeking at tokens not yet generated.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Greedy Decoding (2 marks):**
Greedy decoding selects the single highest-probability token at each step: $y_t^* = \arg\max_v P(v \mid y_{<t})$. It is **fast** and **deterministic** but tends to produce generic or repetitive output, since locally optimal choices are not globally optimal.

**Step 2 — Beam Search (2.5 marks):**
Beam search maintains $k$ candidate sequences (beams) simultaneously and at each step expands all of them by one token, keeping only the top-$k$ partial sequences ranked by cumulative log-probability. Typical $k = 4$ or $5$. Beam search yields higher **BLEU scores** in machine translation but still suffers from generic outputs in open-ended generation.

**Step 3 — Top-$p$ (Nucleus) Sampling (2.5 marks):**
Top-$p$ sampling selects the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds $p$ (e.g., $p = 0.9$) and samples from this truncated distribution. Unlike top-$k$, the size of the candidate set **adapts** to the entropy of the distribution. It produces more diverse, natural-sounding text and is preferred for **chatbots and creative generation**.

**Conclusion:** Greedy/beam are preferred for **constrained, deterministic tasks** (translation, code, factual QA), while top-$p$ sampling is preferred for **open-ended generation** (stories, dialogue).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the temperature-scaled softmax formula. Show mathematically what happens in the limits $\tau \to 0$ and $\tau \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Top-$p$ nucleus sampling algorithm with a numerical example involving a vocabulary of at least 5 tokens. Compute perplexity for a given test sequence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define logits (1 mark):**
Let $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_{\vert V \vert})$ be the raw logits from the LM head.

**Step 2 — Apply temperature scaling (2 marks):**
Divide each logit by $\tau$ to get scaled logits $z_v' = z_v / \tau$.

**Step 3 — Apply softmax (2 marks):**
$$P_\tau(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$$

**Step 4 — Limit $\tau \to 0$ (1 mark):**
The exponential of any finite value divided by a vanishingly small $\tau$ explodes. The largest logit dominates, so $P_\tau(v) \to \delta_{v, v^*}$ where $v^* = \arg\max z_v$. This is **greedy decoding**.

**Step 5 — Limit $\tau \to \infty$ (1 mark):**
All exponentials become $\exp(0) = 1$, so $P_\tau(v) \to 1/\vert V \vert$. The output is **uniformly random**, independent of the model's learned distribution. Quality collapses.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Top-$p$ algorithm description (2 marks):**
(a) Sort all tokens in vocabulary $V$ by descending probability $P(v \mid y_{<t})$.
(b) Compute cumulative sums of sorted probabilities.
(c) Find the smallest prefix set $S_p$ such that $\sum_{v \in S_p} P(v \mid y_{<t}) \geq p$.
(d) Renormalize probabilities within $S_p$ so they sum to 1.
(e) Sample one token from this renormalized distribution.

**Step 2 — Numerical example (3 marks):**
Given probabilities: "rain" = 0.45, "snow" = 0.30, "hail" = 0.12, "sleet" = 0.08, "fog" = 0.05, with $p = 0.85$.
Cumulative: rain → 0.45; rain+snow → 0.75; rain+snow+hail → **0.87** ≥ 0.85. STOP.
Nucleus $S_p = \{\text{rain, snow, hail}\}$. Renormalize:
$P'(\text{rain}) = 0.45/0.87 \approx 0.517$, $P'(\text{snow}) = 0.30/0.87 \approx 0.345$, $P'(\text{hail}) = 0.12/0.87 \approx 0.138$.
Sample one of these three.

**Step 3 — Perplexity computation (2 marks):**
Given $\log P(y_1) = -1.5$, $\log P(y_2) = -2.0$, $\log P(y_3) = -0.5$, with $N=3$:
$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{3}(-1.5 - 2.0 - 0.5) = \frac{4.0}{3} \approx 1.333$.
$\text{PPL} = \exp(1.333) \approx 3.79$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by: (1) **forgetting to renormalize** probabilities after truncating with top-$k$ or top-$p$; (2) **confusing log-probability with probability** in perplexity calculations (always use natural log $\log$, not $\log_{10}$); (3) **omitting the causal mask** discussion when explaining autoregressive generation — the mask is what makes generation *autoregressive*; (4) writing $\vert x \vert$ with vertical pipes inside markdown tables — this breaks formatting in many parsers (use `\vert x \vert` instead).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Text generation = autoregressive token-by-token sampling** from $P(y_t \mid y_{<t})$.
- ✅ **Pipeline:** Tokenize → Embed → Transformer Decoder → LM Head → Decode → Loop until EOS.
- ✅ **Causal mask** is the mechanism enforcing left-to-right generation in transformers.
- ✅ **Greedy decoding** = $\arg\max$; **Beam search** = top-$k$ sequences; **Top-$p$** = nucleus sampling.
- ✅ **Temperature $\tau$** sharpens ($\tau < 1$) or flattens ($\tau > 1$) the softmax distribution.
- ✅ **Perplexity** = $\exp(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log P)$; lower is better.
- ✅ **BLEU** = n-gram precision with brevity penalty; standard for translation evaluation.
- ✅ **Top-$p$** adapts the candidate set size to distribution entropy; preferred over top-$k$ for open-ended tasks.
- ✅ Common defaults: $\tau = 1.0$, $p = 0.9$, beam width $k = 4$ or $5$.
- ✅ **Key formula to memorize:** $P_\tau(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v/\tau)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j/\tau)}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Music Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT756 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Music Composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Music Composition using Generative AI** refers to the automated creation of musical pieces—melodies, harmonies, rhythms, or full multi-instrument arrangements—through deep generative models trained on symbolic (MIDI, MusicXML, ABC notation) or audio-domain (spectrogram, raw waveform) representations of music. It is a subset of **conditional and unconditional sequence/audio generation** under the broader umbrella of Generative AI.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, music composition models are classified along **three axes**:

| Axis | Options |
|------|---------|
| Output Domain | **Symbolic** (notes/tokens) vs **Audio** (waveform/spectrogram) |
| Conditioning | **Unconditional** vs **Conditional** (text, image, lyrics, chord, style) |
| Architecture | **Autoregressive** (RNN/LSTM/Transformer) vs **Latent Diffusion** vs **GAN** vs **VAE** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Composer's Rehearsal Room"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a chef who has never tasted food but has read 10 million recipe books. He cannot *feel* taste, but he statistically knows that *chicken + soy + ginger* usually follows *garlic + onion*. A generative music model behaves similarly—it learns the **statistical grammar of music** (which notes follow which, in what rhythm, in what key) from millions of songs, then samples new sequences that obey those learned rules.

**Plain-English intuition:**
- The model does **not** "understand" music emotionally. It models $P(\text{next token} \mid \text{previous tokens, conditioning})$.
- A **token** in symbolic music ≈ a discrete event (e.g., `pitch=60`, `velocity=90`, `duration=0.5s`, `time=4.2s`).
- A **spectrogram frame** in audio music ≈ a 2D image of frequency-vs-time intensity, treated like a pixel image for diffusion models.

---

## 1.3 Why Music Composition is Hard for AI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Music is **multi-scale**, **multi-modal**, and **hierarchically structured**. A single 4-minute pop song contains structure across **at least four temporal scales** simultaneously:

$$
\text{Structure:} \quad \text{Song} \rightarrow \text{Section} \rightarrow \text{Bar} \rightarrow \text{Beat} \rightarrow \text{Note}
$$

A robust generative model must respect:
1. **Local coherence** — adjacent notes must form a valid scale/chord (millisecond scale).
2. **Phrase-level grammar** — melodic contour must form a singable line (second scale).
3. **Harmonic progression** — chord changes follow functional harmony (bar scale).
4. **Long-term form** — verse–chorus–bridge repetition (minute scale).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why naïve LSTM models fail on long pieces but **Transformers** and **hierarchical tokenizers** (like MuPe-GAN's beat-level tokens) succeed.

---

## 1.4 Key Milestone Models (KTU High-Yield)

| Year | Model | Paradigm | Output Type |
|------|-------|----------|-------------|
| 2016 | DeepBach (Hadjeres) | RNN/LSTM | Symbolic (4-part Bach chorales) |
| 2017 | Google Magenta (NSynth, MelodyRNN) | RNN + WaveNet autoencoder | Symbolic + Audio |
| 2018 | MuseGAN (Dong et al.) | **GAN** on piano-roll | Multi-track symbolic |
| 2020 | Jukebox (OpenAI) | VQ-VAE + Sparse Transformer | Raw audio (multi-instrument) |
| 2023 | MusicGen (Meta) | **Transformer + EnCodec codec** | Audio (text-conditioned) |
| 2023 | AudioLDM / AudioLDM 2 | **Latent Diffusion** | Audio (text-conditioned) |
| 2024 | Stable Audio | Latent Diffusion (commercial) | Audio (text-conditioned) |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Symbolic Music Representation

### 2.1.1 MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface)
MIDI is **NOT audio**. It is a symbolic event protocol consisting of discrete messages:

$$
\text{MIDI Event} = (t_{\text{on}}, \text{pitch}, \text{velocity}, \text{channel})
$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{on}}$ = onset time in ticks (typically 480 ticks/beat)
- $\text{pitch} \in \{0, 1, \dots, 127\}$ — MIDI note number
- $\text{velocity} \in \{1, \dots, 127\}$ — loudness
- $\text{channel} \in \{0, \dots, 15\}$ — instrument

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** MIDI files are ~1 KB/minute vs ~10 MB/minute for MP3, making them ideal training data for symbolic models like Music Transformer.

### 2.1.2 Piano Roll Representation
A **piano roll** is a 2D binary/continuous tensor:

$$
\mathbf{X} \in \{0,1\}^{T \times 88} \quad \text{(or } \mathbb{R}^{T \times 128}\text{ for full MIDI range)}
$$

- Rows = time steps (e.g., 16th-note grid)
- Columns = pitches
- $X[t, p] = 1$ if pitch $p$ is sounding at time $t$.

MuseGAN treats multi-track piano rolls as **stacked 2D images** fed to convolutional GANs.

---

## 2.2 MusicGen — Meta's Transformer-on-Codec Approach

**MusicGen** (Copet et al., 2023) is the canonical KTU reference for modern audio music generation.

### 2.2.1 Architecture Pipeline

$$
\text{Text} \;\xrightarrow{\text{T5 encoder}}\; \mathbf{C}_t \;\xrightarrow{\text{Cross-Attention}}\; \text{Transformer Decoder} \;\rightarrow\; z_1, z_2, \dots, z_K \;\xrightarrow{\text{EnCodec Decoder}}\; \text{Waveform}
$$

### 2.2.2 Three-Stage Process

| Stage | Module | Purpose |
|-------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **EnCodec** (24 kHz, 50 Hz) | Compresses audio waveform $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{T_w}$ into discrete codec tokens $z_i \in \{0, \dots, 2047\}^{K}$ |
| 2 | **Transformer Decoder** | Autoregressively models $P(z_i \mid z_{<i}, \mathbf{C}_t)$ |
| 3 | **EnCodec Decoder** | Reconstructs waveform from predicted codec tokens |

---

## 2.3 Latent Diffusion for Audio (AudioLDM)

### 2.3.1 Forward (Noise) Process
For a latent $\mathbf{z}_0 \sim p(\mathbf{z})$ (mel-spectrogram encoded by VAE), diffusion adds Gaussian noise over $T$ steps:

$$
\mathbf{z}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{z}_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})
$$

Where $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s$ and $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$.

### 2.3.2 Reverse (Denoising) Process
A neural network $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{z}_t, t, \mathbf{c})$ learns to predict the noise, conditioned on text embedding $\mathbf{c}$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, \mathbf{z}_0, \boldsymbol{\epsilon}}\left[\,\|\boldsymbol{\epsilon} - \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{z}_t, t, \mathbf{c})\|_2^2\,\right]
$$

---

## 2.4 MuseGAN — Multi-Track GAN

MuseGAN generates **$B$ bars of $P$-track music** as a tensor:

$$
\mathbf{X} \in \{0, 1\}^{B \times P \times R \times 88}
$$

Where:
- $B$ = number of bars
- $P$ = number of tracks (drums, bass, chord, melody, etc.)
- $R$ = time resolution (e.g., 16 steps/bar)
- $88$ = piano keys

### 2.4.1 Generator Hierarchy

$$
\mathbf{z}_{\text{global}} \in \mathbb{R}^{128} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Generator}}\; \mathbf{X}
$$

A **chord-conditioned** variant uses:
$$
\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times 36} \quad (\text{one-hot chord per bar, 36 chord types})
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | MIDI pitch → frequency | $f = 440 \cdot 2^{(p-69)/12}$ Hz | $p=69$ is A4 |
| 2 | Velocity → amplitude | $A \approx 10^{V/127}$ (rough) | Logarithmic perception |
| 3 | Diffusion forward | $\mathbf{z}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{z}_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ | $\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$ |
| 4 | Diffusion loss | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}\left[\vert\vert \boldsymbol{\epsilon} - \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{z}_t,t,\mathbf{c}) \vert\vert_2^2\right]$ | Simple MSE objective |
| 5 | Autoregressive log-likelihood | $\log P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})$ | NLL training objective |
| 6 | GAN minimax (JS) | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$ | MuseGAN objective |
| 7 | Spectral convergence (eval) | $\mathrm{SC} = \frac{\vert\vert \vert S \vert - \vert \hat{S} \vert \vert\vert_F}{\vert\vert \vert S \vert \vert\vert_F}$ | Audio quality metric |
| 8 | Frechet Audio Distance | $\mathrm{FAD} = \vert\vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert\vert^2 + \mathrm{Tr}\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r\Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | Inception-style for audio |
| 9 | Beat-tracking F1 | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Rhythm accuracy |
| 10 | Cross-entropy (symbolic) | $H = -\sum_{c} p(c)\log p(c)$ | Per-token training loss |

---

## 2.6 Why Each Architecture is Chosen — Engineering Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner frequently asks "Why Transformer over RNN for music?"**

| Architecture | Pros | Cons | Best For |
|--------------|------|------|----------|
| RNN/LSTM | Sequential inductive bias | Vanishing gradient, slow | Short monophonic melodies |
| Transformer | Parallel training, long context | Quadratic memory in $N$ | Mid-length symbolic pieces |
| Hierarchical Transformer | Multi-scale structure | Complex tokenization | Long-form compositions |
| GAN (MuseGAN) | Fast inference | Mode collapse, unstable | Multi-track piano-rolls |
| Diffusion (AudioLDM) | High fidelity, stable | Slow iterative sampling | Studio-quality audio |
| VQ-VAE + Transformer | Discrete latent, scalable | Reconstruction ceiling | Jukebox-style raw audio |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: MIDI Pitch to Frequency Mapping

The equal-tempered scale divides each octave into 12 equal logarithmic steps.

**Step 1** — Define the reference. A4 = MIDI pitch 69 = 440 Hz.

**Step 2** — Each semitone differs by a factor of $2^{1/12}$ in frequency.

**Step 3** — A pitch $p$ is $p - 69$ semitones away from A4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(p) &= 440 \cdot 2^{(p-69)/12} \\[4pt]
\text{Example: } p = 81 \;(\text{A5}) &\Rightarrow f = 440 \cdot 2^{12/12} = 440 \cdot 2 = 880 \text{ Hz} \\[4pt]
\text{Example: } p = 60 \;(\text{C4, middle C}) &\Rightarrow f = 440 \cdot 2^{-9/12} \approx 261.63 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is foundational because many symbolic music models output **pitch tokens**, and downstream audio synthesizers must convert them to frequencies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Forward Diffusion Process for Audio Spectrograms

The forward diffusion is a **fixed Markov chain** that gradually destroys signal.

**Step 1** — Define noise schedule $\beta_t$, with $\beta_t \in (0,1)$, typically linearly increasing from $\beta_1 = 10^{-4}$ to $\beta_T = 0.02$.

**Step 2** — One-step transition:
$$
q(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_t;\, \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,\mathbf{z}_{t-1},\, \beta_t \mathbf{I})
$$

**Step 3** — Closed-form marginal for any $t$:
$$
q(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_t;\, \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{z}_0,\, (1-\bar{\alpha}_t)\mathbf{I})
$$

Where $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}(1-\beta_s)$.

**Step 4** — Reparameterize:
$$
\mathbf{z}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{z}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})
$$

**Step 5** — At $t=T$: $\bar{\alpha}_T \approx 0$, so $\mathbf{z}_T \approx \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ (pure noise).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **why** diffusion models need $T$ reverse steps: each denoising step $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta$ removes only a small slice of noise.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: MusicGen Autoregressive Training Objective

Let $\mathbf{Z} = (z_1, z_2, \dots, z_N)$ be the sequence of codec tokens, $\mathbf{C}_t$ the text embedding.

**Step 1** — Joint probability factorization (chain rule):
$$
P(\mathbf{Z} \mid \mathbf{C}_t) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(z_i \mid z_{<i}, \mathbf{C}_t)
$$

**Step 2** — Training loss = negative log-likelihood:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(z_i \mid z_{<i}, \mathbf{C}_t)
$$

**Step 3** — In practice, cross-entropy over the EnCodec codebook of size $K=2048$:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{AR}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}[z_i = k]\,\log\frac{\exp(\mathbf{h}_i^\top \mathbf{e}_k)}{\sum_{k'}\exp(\mathbf{h}_i^\top \mathbf{e}_{k'})}
$$

Where $\mathbf{h}_i$ is the Transformer hidden state at position $i$, $\mathbf{e}_k$ is the codebook embedding.

**Step 4** — Inference uses **top-$k$ sampling** or **classifier-free guidance**:
$$
\tilde{\ell}_i = (1+w)\,\ell_i(\mathbf{C}_t) - w\,\ell_i(\varnothing)
$$
Where $w$ is the guidance scale (typically $w=3$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: MuseGAN Loss (Minimax)

MuseGAN uses the standard GAN objective on piano-roll "images".

**Step 1** — Generator $G: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ maps random noise $\mathbf{z}$ to piano-roll $\mathbf{X}$.

**Step 2** — Discriminator $D: \mathcal{X} \to [0,1]$ classifies real vs fake bars.

**Step 3** — Value function:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \;&\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{X} \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(\mathbf{X})] \\
+ \;&\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Optimal discriminator: $D^*(\mathbf{X}) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{X})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{X}) + p_G(\mathbf{X})}$.

**Step 5** — Substituting gives Jensen-Shannon divergence minimization:
$$
V(D^*,G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \mathrm{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_G)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MuseGAN uses temporal jittering + random transposition as augmentation:** to prevent the discriminator from overfitting to absolute pitch/time, forcing $G$ to learn invariant musical structure.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Frechet Audio Distance (FAD)

FAD is the gold-standard objective metric for music generation.

**Step 1** — Extract embeddings $\mathbf{f}_r, \mathbf{f}_g \in \mathbb{R}^d$ from a pretrained audio classifier (e.g., VGGish, PANN) for real and generated sets.

**Step 2** — Compute mean $\mu_r, \mu_g$ and covariance $\Sigma_r, \Sigma_g$.

**Step 3** — Frechet distance:
$$
\mathrm{FAD} = \vert\vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert\vert^2 + \mathrm{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)
$$

**Step 4** — Lower FAD $\Rightarrow$ generated distribution is statistically closer to real.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FAD is **not perceptual**—it correlates with but does not equal human preference. KTU exam answers should always pair FAD with a subjective MOS (Mean Opinion Score) discussion.
