# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between symbolic and audio-domain music generation. Give one example model for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Symbolic | Audio |
|--------|----------|-------|
| Output | Notes, MIDI events | Spectrogram / raw waveform |
| Representation | Discrete tokens / piano roll | Continuous 2D / 1D signal |
| Example model | **MuseGAN** (Dong et al., 2018) | **MusicGen** (Meta, 2023) |
| Strength | Interpretable, editable | Studio-ready, no synth needed |
| Weakness | Requires downstream synth | Hard to edit, opaque |

**(1 mark** for clear distinction, **1 mark** for examples, **1 mark** for strength/weakness.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of EnCodec in the MusicGen architecture?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EnCodec is a **neural audio codec** that performs two functions inside MusicGen:

1. **Encoding:** Compresses raw 24 kHz audio waveform into discrete codec tokens at 50 Hz frame rate. Each token is drawn from a codebook of size **2048**. This reduces a 30-second clip from $\sim$720,000 samples to **1500 tokens**, making autoregressive modeling tractable. (1.5 marks)

2. **Decoding:** Reconstructs the waveform from predicted codec tokens during inference, enabling end-to-end text-to-audio output. (1 mark)

3. **Why it matters:** Without EnCodec, the Transformer would have to model raw 24 kHz samples (30 seconds = 720k steps), which is computationally infeasible. EnCodec bridges the gap between audio fidelity and sequence-model tractability. (0.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) confusing *symbolic* vs *audio* generation, (b) omitting the **conditioning mechanism** in conditional models, (c) skipping the **evaluation metric discussion**, and (d) drawing architecture diagrams without labeling tensor shapes. Always include tensor dimensions and at least one equation per 7-mark sub-part.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **MuseGAN** for multi-track music generation. Discuss how the chord, style, melody, and drum generators collaborate to produce a coherent multi-track piano roll. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the MuseGAN training objective from the standard GAN minimax formulation and explain why **temporal jittering** and **random transposition** are used as data augmentation during discriminator training. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution A(a):

**Step 1 — Problem Formulation (1 mark):**
MuseGAN treats music as a multi-track piano-roll tensor:
$$
\mathbf{X} \in \{0,1\}^{B \times P \times R \times 88}
$$
With $B=4$ bars, $P=4$ tracks (drums, bass, chord, melody), $R=16$ steps/bar.

**Step 2 — Four Sub-Generators (3 marks):**
- **Chord Generator** $G_c$: input $\mathbf{z}_c \in \mathbb{R}^{100}$, output chord progression tensor.
- **Style Generator** $G_s$: input $\mathbf{z}_s \in \mathbb{R}^{100}$, outputs a shared style latent repeated across bars.
- **Melody Generator** $G_m$: input $(\mathbf{z}_m, \text{chord})$, outputs melody track conditioned on chord.
- **Drum Generator** $G_d$: input $\mathbf{z}_d$, outputs drum track (rhythmic, less harmonically constrained).

**Step 3 — Inter-Generator Conditioning (2 marks):**
The melody generator conditions on the **chord tensor** produced by $G_c$ via concatenation along the channel axis before transposed convolution, enforcing harmonic compatibility. The **style latent** $\mathbf{z}_s$ is broadcast across all bars and tracks, ensuring stylistic consistency.

**Step 4 — Discriminator (1 mark):**
A CNN-based discriminator $D$ operates on each $(B/2, P, R, 88)$ sliding window, classifying real vs. fake. Two variants exist: **bar-wise** (independent judgement per bar) and **track-wise** (judges each track separately).

#### Model Solution A(b):

**Step 1 — Standard GAN Objective (2 marks):**
$$
\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}\sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]
$$

**Step 2 — Derive Optimal Discriminator (2 marks):**
Setting $\partial V / \partial D = 0$:
$$
D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_G(\mathbf{x})}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back (2 marks):**
$$
V(D^*,G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \mathrm{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_G)
$$
The generator minimizes the Jensen-Shannon divergence between real and generated distributions.

**Step 4 — Why Augmentation (1 mark):**
- **Temporal jittering** shifts piano rolls by ±1–2 time steps, preventing $D$ from memorizing absolute timing and forcing $G$ to learn tempo-invariant musical structure.
- **Random transposition** shifts pitches by ±2–5 semitones, preventing $D$ from using absolute pitch as a shortcut and forcing $G$ to learn *relative* melodic/harmonic relationships.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **MusicGen** architecture for text-conditioned music generation. Explain the role of the EnCodec codec and the cross-attention mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward diffusion process** for latent audio diffusion (AudioLDM). Show how the closed-form marginal $q(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_0)$ is obtained using the reparameterization trick. Also explain why diffusion models outperform GANs in audio fidelity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B(a):

**Step 1 — Pipeline (2 marks):**
$$\text{Text} \xrightarrow{\text{T5}} \mathbf{C}_t \quad;\quad \text{Real Audio} \xrightarrow{\text{EnCodec-Enc}} (z_1,\dots,z_N) \quad;\quad \text{Transformer-Dec}(z_{<i},\mathbf{C}_t) \rightarrow z_i$$
Tokens decoded back to audio via EnCodec-Dec.

**Step 2 — EnCodec Role (2 marks):**
Compresses 24 kHz audio into **discrete tokens** at 50 Hz with codebook of 2048 entries. Reduces sequence length by ~480×, making autoregressive modeling tractable. Also acts as a **high-fidelity neural synthesizer** during inference.

**Step 3 — Cross-Attention Mechanism (2 marks):**
Each Transformer decoder block has:
$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V
$$
Where $Q$ = decoder hidden states (music tokens), $K,V$ = projections of text embedding $\mathbf{C}_t$. This injects semantic text conditioning into every generation step.

**Step 4 — Why Decoder-only (1 mark):**
Music generation is a **monotonic** sequence task — every future token depends only on past tokens + conditioning. A decoder-only Transformer (à la GPT) is sufficient; no encoder-decoder cross-attention between input modalities is needed.

#### Model Solution B(b):

**Step 1 — Forward Diffusion Definition (2 marks):**
$$
q(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_t;\, \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,\mathbf{z}_{t-1},\, \beta_t \mathbf{I})
$$

**Step 2 — Reparameterized One-Step (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbf{z}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{z}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}(1-\beta_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Marginal $q(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_0)$ (2 marks):**
By induction and the properties of Gaussian composition:
$$
q(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_t;\, \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{z}_0,\, (1-\bar{\alpha}_t)\mathbf{I})
$$
This **closed form** eliminates the need to iterate through all $t$ steps during training — sampling one $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ and applying the formula suffices.

**Step 4 — Why Diffusion Beats GAN for Audio (1 mark):**
- GANs suffer from **mode collapse** and **spectral artifacts** in high-dimensional audio (44.1 kHz × 30 s = 1.3M dimensions).
- Diffusion's **iterative refinement** ensures mode coverage and produces smoother spectrograms without the high-frequency checkerboard artifacts typical of transposed-convolution GAN upsamplers.
- Empirically, AudioLDM achieves **FAD ≈ 1.97** vs MuseGAN's piano-roll reconstruction artifacts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme PERUT756 Module 4 — Music Composition**

- **Symbolic vs Audio:** Symbolic = MIDI / piano-roll (MuseGAN, DeepBach). Audio = waveform / spectrogram (MusicGen, AudioLDM).
- **MIDI Pitch formula:** $f = 440 \cdot 2^{(p-69)/12}$ Hz. A4 = 69 = 440 Hz.
- **Piano roll:** Tensor $\mathbf{X} \in \{0,1\}^{T \times 88}$ — time × pitch grid.
- **MuseGAN:** 4 sub-generators (chord, style, melody, drum), CNN discriminator, JSD minimization, augmentation = temporal jitter + transposition.
- **MusicGen:** T5 text encoder + Transformer decoder + EnCodec codec. 24 kHz, 50 Hz tokens, codebook 2048. Cross-attention injects text conditioning.
- **EnCodec:** Neural audio codec. **Encodes** audio → discrete tokens. **Decodes** tokens → waveform. Enables tractable sequence modeling.
- **AudioLDM:** Latent diffusion on mel-spectrogram. VAE encoder → UNet denoiser → VAE decoder. Text conditioned via CLAP.
- **Forward diffusion:** $\mathbf{z}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\mathbf{z}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$. Closed-form marginal exists.
- **Training loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}[\vert\vert \boldsymbol{\epsilon} - \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{z}_t, t, \mathbf{c}) \vert\vert_2^2]$ (simple MSE).
- **Autoregressive loss:** NLL = $-\sum_i \log P(z_i \mid z_{<i}, \mathbf{C}_t)$.
- **Classifier-free guidance:** $\tilde{\ell} = (1+w)\ell(\mathbf{c}) - w\ell(\varnothing)$; typical $w=3$.
- **FAD (Frechet Audio Distance):** Gold-standard objective metric using pretrained embeddings; lower is better.
- **MOS (Mean Opinion Score):** Subjective 1–5 human rating; required alongside FAD for full evaluation.
- **Hierarchy:** Note → Beat → Bar → Section → Song. Multi-scale modeling is the key open challenge.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never confuse **EnCodec** (audio codec) with **VAE** (latent encoder in diffusion); they serve different roles.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Always mention **conditioning** when describing a conditional generative model; omitting it costs marks.
- **KTU favorite question:** "Compare autoregressive vs diffusion for music generation" — answer with table covering fidelity, speed, mode coverage, training stability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Healthcare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT756 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Healthcare

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Generative AI in Healthcare** refers to the application of deep generative models — including *Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)*, *Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)*, *Diffusion Models*, *Large Language Models (LLMs)*, and *Transformer-based architectures* — to **synthesize, augment, interpret, and reason** over complex biomedical and clinical data such as medical imaging, electronic health records (EHR), molecular structures, and clinical narratives.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** curriculum framing, this domain sits at the intersection of *computational medicine*, *medical informatics*, and *applied deep learning*, forming a critical *high-impact vertical* of Generative AI deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The Healthcare vertical is mandated as a *Domain-Specific Generative Application* under Module 4. Students must demonstrate awareness of *medical data modalities*, *regulatory constraints (HIPAA/GDPR)*, *clinical evaluation metrics*, and *ethical considerations* specific to life-critical AI deployment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Twin Doctor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a master artist who has studied **10 million** patient X-rays and can now paint a *plausible new X-ray* of a hypothetical patient, or translate a fuzzy CT scan into a crystal-clear MRI — that is essentially what a generative AI model does. It learns the *underlying statistical distribution* of medical data and can *sample*, *transform*, or *generate* new samples that obey the same medical reality.

Think of a **Generative AI system in healthcare** like a *medical forensic artist*:
- It studies thousands of historical patient cases (training data).
- It learns the *anatomy of normalcy* and the *morphology of disease*.
- When given a partial or noisy input (e.g., a low-dose CT scan), it reconstructs, enhances, or forecasts the most likely medically-correct counterpart.

## 1.3 Core Healthcare Data Modalities

| Modality | Description | Typical Format | Generative AI Application |
|----------|-------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| **Medical Imaging** | X-ray, CT, MRI, PET, Ultrasound | 2D/3D Voxel Arrays, DICOM | Super-resolution, Cross-modal synthesis |
| **Electronic Health Records (EHR)** | Patient demographics, history, lab results | Structured/Unstructured Text | Synthetic patient generation, imputation |
| **Clinical Text** | Discharge summaries, radiology reports, clinical notes | Free-text NLP corpora | Report generation, summarization |
| **Molecular Data** | SMILES strings, protein sequences, 3D conformations | Strings, 3D Point Clouds | Drug discovery, protein folding |
| **Genomic Data** | DNA/RNA sequences | String sequences | Sequence generation, variant prediction |
| **Signal Data** | ECG, EEG, EMG waveforms | 1D Time Series | Anomaly synthesis, denoising |

## 1.4 Why Generative AI Matters in Healthcare

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Triple Bottleneck Problem in Medicine:**
> 1. **Data Scarcity:** Rare diseases have insufficient real patient samples.
> 2. **Privacy Constraints:** HIPAA, GDPR, and ABDM (India) restrict patient data sharing.
> 3. **Annotation Cost:** Expert radiologist labelling costs roughly **\$50–\$200 per image**.

Generative AI solves all three simultaneously by creating *synthetic yet realistic* data that preserves statistical properties without exposing patient identity.

## 1.5 Ethical & Regulatory Boundary Conditions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Healthcare AI is a *safety-critical* domain. The KTU Module 4 explicitly expects students to recognize:
> - **HIPAA** (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, USA)
> - **GDPR** (General Data Protection Regulation, EU)
> - **ABDM** (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission, India)
> - **FDA SaMD** (Software as a Medical Device) classification frameworks
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Taxonomy for Healthcare Generative AI

### 2.1.1 Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) — Medical Imaging Backbone

The **GAN** framework, originally proposed for natural images, has become the workhorse for medical image synthesis. It consists of two neural networks in *adversarial equilibrium*:

- **Generator (G):** Maps a latent noise vector $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ to a synthetic medical image.
- **Discriminator (D):** Distinguishes real medical images from generated (fake) ones.

The **Minimax Objective Function** is given by:

$$
\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]
$$

**Healthcare Variants of GANs:**

| Variant | Full Form | Healthcare Use Case |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| **pix2pix** | Image-to-Image Translation GAN | CT $\to$ MRI cross-modal synthesis |
| **CycleGAN** | Cycle-Consistent GAN | Unpaired modality translation |
| **StyleGAN** | Style-based GAN | Synthetic skin lesion generation |
| **MedGAN** | Medical GAN | EHR synthetic data generation |
| **ProGAN** | Progressive GAN | High-resolution histopathology |

### 2.1.2 Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) — Probabilistic Backbone

A VAE learns a *probabilistic latent representation* of medical data through an **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** maximization. This is particularly valuable for:
- **Anomaly detection** (e.g., tumor vs. normal tissue)
- **Data imputation** for missing EHR fields
- **Controlled generation** via latent space traversal

The ELBO objective is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \;\Vert\; p(z))
$$

### 2.1.3 Diffusion Models — State-of-the-Art Generators

Diffusion models learn to *reverse a gradual noising process*, generating samples by *iterative denoising*. They currently dominate:
- **Medical image synthesis** (e.g., CheX Diffusion)
- **Anatomy-aware generation** for surgical planning
- **Low-dose CT denoising**

The **forward noising process** is defined as a Markov chain:

$$
q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\left(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1}, \beta_t I\right)
$$

The **reverse denoising process** is parameterized as:

$$
p_{\theta}(x_{t-1} \mid x_t) = \mathcal{N}\left(x_{t-1}; \mu_{\theta}(x_t, t), \Sigma_{\theta}(x_t, t)\right)
$$

### 2.1.4 Transformer-based LLMs — Clinical Text Backbone

Models like **BioBERT**, **ClinicalBERT**, **Med-PaLM**, **GatorTron**, and **GPT-4 Med** are fine-tuned on biomedical corpora (PubMed, MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV) for:

- **Radiology report generation** (image $\to$ text)
- **Clinical decision support** (text $\to$ recommendation)
- **Drug-drug interaction extraction**
- **Medical question answering**

The autoregressive generation objective is:

$$
p(y \mid x) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p_{\theta}(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)
$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Equation / Value | Units / Domain | Application |
|--------|------|------------------|----------------|-------------|
| $\mathcal{L}_G$ | GAN Generator Loss | $-\mathbb{E}_{z}[\log D(G(z))]$ | dimensionless | Adversarial training |
| $\mathcal{L}_D$ | GAN Discriminator Loss | $-\left(\mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]\right)$ | dimensionless | Real/fake classification |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}$ | VAE Loss | $\mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q \Vert p)$ | nats | Latent representation |
| $D_{\text{KL}}$ | KL Divergence | $\sum p(x) \log(p(x)/q(x))$ | nats | Distribution matching |
| $\beta_t$ | Diffusion Noise Schedule | linear/cosine, $0 < \beta_1 < \beta_T < 1$ | scalar | Noise injection rate |
| $\bar{\alpha}_t$ | Cumulative Product | $\prod_{s=1}^{t}(1-\beta_s)$ | scalar | Forward process |
| $L_{\text{recon}}$ | Reconstruction Loss | $\Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert_2^2$ or BCE | image-domain | Image fidelity |
| $\text{FID}$ | Fréchet Inception Distance | $\Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r\Sigma_g)^{1/2})$ | scalar (lower is better) | Generative image quality |
| $\text{SSIM}$ | Structural Similarity Index | $\frac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + c_2)}{(\mu_x^2+\mu_y^2+c_1)(\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+c_2)}$ | $[0,1]$, higher better | Medical image fidelity |
| $\text{PSNR}$ | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $10 \log_{10}(\text{MAX}^2 / \text{MSE})$ | dB | Image reconstruction quality |
| $\text{Dice}$ | Dice Similarity Coefficient | $\frac{2 \vert X \cap Y \vert}{\vert X \vert + \vert Y \vert}$ | $[0,1]$, higher better | Segmentation overlap |
| $\text{BLEU}$ | Bilingual Evaluation Understudy | $\text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_n w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $[0,1]$ | Clinical text generation |
| $\text{ROUGE-L}$ | Recall-Oriented Understudy | $F_{\text{measure}}$ on LCS | $[0,1]$ | Report summarization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Notice how the formula sheet uses $\Vert$ (double vertical bars) only when written in display math. When placed inside markdown tables, we use the word-form `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table syntax breakage. Always memorize the **FID and Dice** formulae — they are the two most-tested metrics in KTU Healthcare Module questions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Generative AI Solution | Clinical Impact |
|--------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Radiology** | GAN-based CT $\to$ MRI translation | Eliminates redundant scans, reduces radiation exposure |
| **Pathology** | Diffusion-based histopathology synthesis | Balances rare-class datasets |
| **Pharmacy** | Reinforcement Learning + Transformer for molecular generation | Accelerates drug discovery from years to months |
| **Mental Health** | LLM-based therapeutic chatbots | 24/7 scalable mental health support |
| **Surgery** | 3D diffusion-based anatomy reconstruction | Pre-operative planning |
| **Coding & Billing** | LLM-based clinical note generation | Reduces physician burnout by $\approx 40\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reference:** *JPMorgan Research (2023)* estimated Generative AI could unlock **\$200–360 billion** annually across the healthcare value chain, with drug discovery and clinical operations being the two largest contributors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for VAE in Medical Imaging

> **Context:** Used for synthetic medical image generation, anomaly detection, and latent-space-based controlled generation.

### Step 1 — Define the Generative Process

We assume a medical image $x$ (e.g., a chest X-ray) is generated by first sampling a latent code $z$ from a prior distribution $p(z)$, then sampling $x$ from the conditional likelihood $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$:

$$
x \sim p_{\theta}(x \mid z), \quad z \sim p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Marginal Likelihood

Our goal is to maximize $\log p_{\theta}(x)$. Since $p_{\theta}(x) = \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p(z) dz$ is *intractable*, we introduce an approximate posterior $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p(z) dz
$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the Importance Sampling Trick

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)} dz
$$

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Jensen's Inequality

Since $\log$ is concave, $\log \mathbb{E}[Y] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log Y]$. Applying this:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\left[\log \frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Expand the Expectation

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] + \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\left[\log \frac{p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right]
$$

### Step 6 — Identify the KL Divergence Term

By definition, $D_{\text{KL}}(q \Vert p) = \mathbb{E}_{q}[\log(q/p)]$, so:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))
$$

### Step 7 — Final ELBO Expression

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction Term}} - \underbrace{D_{\text{KL}}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))}_{\text{Regularization Term}}
$$

> **Clinical Interpretation:** The first term forces the VAE to *reconstruct* the medical image faithfully (e.g., preserve tumor morphology). The second term forces the latent distribution to remain close to a standard normal, ensuring *smooth latent interpolation* — critical for generating *novel* synthetic samples for rare diseases.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Diffusion Model Forward Process for Medical Image Denoising

> **Context:** Used for low-dose CT denoising, MRI super-resolution, and conditional medical image synthesis.

### Step 1 — Define the Discrete Forward Diffusion

Given a clean medical image $x_0$ (e.g., full-dose CT), we add Gaussian noise in $T$ steps to obtain $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$
q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\left(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1}, \beta_t I\right)
$$

where $\beta_t \in (0, 1)$ is the variance schedule.

### Step 2 — Apply Reparameterization Trick

Let $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$ and $\epsilon_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$
x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t}\, x_{t-1} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}
$$

### Step 3 — Marginalize Over Intermediate Steps

Using the Markov property, $x_t$ can be written *directly* from $x_0$:

$$
x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \text{where } \bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Reverse Denoising (Generative) Process

The model learns to predict the noise $\epsilon$ added at each step:

$$
p_{\theta}(x_{t-1} \mid x_t) = \mathcal{N}\left(x_{t-1}; \mu_{\theta}(x_t, t), \Sigma_{\theta}(x_t, t)\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Simplified Training Objective

A practical simplification (Ho et al., 2020) reduces the loss to predicting the noise:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(x_t, t) \Vert^2\right]
$$

where $\epsilon_{\theta}$ is the neural network's noise prediction.

> **Clinical Interpretation:** A diffusion model trained on full-dose CT scans can be used to denoise *low-dose* CT scans at inference time — reducing patient radiation exposure by up to **$75\%$** while preserving diagnostic fidelity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dice Loss for Medical Image Segmentation

> **Context:** Used to evaluate segmentation overlap between predicted and ground-truth anatomical structures.

### Step 1 — Define Intersection and Union

Let $P$ = predicted segmentation mask, $G$ = ground truth mask:

$$
\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}
$$

### Step 2 — Soft Dice for Differentiable Training

For training, the soft Dice Loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_i p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_i p_i + \sum_i g_i + \epsilon}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small smoothing constant (e.g., $10^{-6}$) to avoid division by zero.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Reference of Architecture, Application, and Evaluation

| Architecture | Medical Task | Input Modality | Output Modality | Primary Metric | Secondary Metric |
|--------------|--------------|----------------|-----------------|----------------|------------------|
| **GAN (pix2pix)** | CT $\to$ MRI translation | CT slice | MRI slice | SSIM | PSNR, FID |
| **CycleGAN** | Unpaired modality synthesis | MRI | CT | FID | SSIM |
| **VAE** | Anomaly detection | X-ray | Reconstruction + Heatmap | AUC-ROC | Reconstruction MSE |
| **Diffusion** | Low-dose CT denoising | Noisy CT | Denoised CT | PSNR | SSIM, RMSE |
| **Transformer LLM** | Radiology report generation | Chest X-ray | Free-text report | BLEU-4 | ROUGE-L, CIDEr |
| **Graph Neural Net** | Drug-drug interaction | Molecular graphs | Interaction probability | AUPRC | F1-Score |
| **RL + Transformer** | De novo drug design | Sequence/SMILES | Novel molecule | QED, SA Score | Binding affinity |
| **3D U-Net + VAE** | Tumor segmentation | 3D MRI volume | Voxel mask | Dice Score | Hausdorff Distance |
| **DDPM** | Synthetic histopathology | Random noise | H&E patch | FID | Inception Score |
| **BioBERT** | Clinical NER | Discharge summary | Entity-tagged text | F1 (entity) | Precision, Recall |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Computing FID Score

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A research team generates 1000 synthetic chest X-rays using a GAN. The FID between real and synthetic feature distributions is reported as: $\mu_r = (1.2, 0.8)$, $\mu_g = (1.5, 1.1)$, $\Sigma_r = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix}$, $\Sigma_g = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.3 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the FID score.

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean Difference Norm**

$$
\Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 = (1.2 - 1.5)^2 + (0.8 - 1.1)^2 = 0.09 + 0.09 = 0.18
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Trace Term**

$$
\text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g) = (0.5 + 0.4) + (0.4 + 0.3) = 0.9 + 0.7 = 1.6
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Matrix Square Root Term**

$$
(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.43 & 0.07 \\ 0.07 & 0.34 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \text{Tr}\left((\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right) \approx 0.77
$$

**Step 4 — Combine**

$$
\text{FID} = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right) \approx 0.18 + (1.6 - 1.54) = 0.24
$$

**Interpretation:** A FID of **$0.24$** indicates *very high* similarity between real and synthetic distributions — clinically promising for use as augmentation data.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Healthcare Generative AI Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Medical Data Ingestion] --> B[Modality Detection]
    B --> C[Preprocessing & DICOM Parsing]
    C --> D[Privacy Filter - HIPAA De-identification]
    D --> E[Architecture Selection Layer]
    E --> F1[GAN Module]
    E --> F2[VAE Module]
    E --> F3[Diffusion Module]
    E --> F4[Transformer LLM Module]
    F1 --> G1[Image Synthesis]
    F2 --> G2[Anomaly Detection]
    F3 --> G3[Image Reconstruction]
    F4 --> G4[Clinical Text Generation]
    G1 --> H[Evaluation Layer]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Clinical Validation]
    I --> J[Deployment - FDA SaMD Review]
```

## 4.2 CT to MRI Cross-Modal Synthesis (pix2pix GAN Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CT[Input CT Scan] --> E1[CT Encoder]
    E1 --> B1[Latent Bottleneck]
    B1 --> D1[MRI Decoder]
    D1 --> MRISynth[Synthetic MRI Output]
    MRISynth --> D2[Discriminator Network]
    MRIReal[Real MRI Reference] --> D2
    D2 --> Loss[Adversarial Loss Calculation]
    Loss --> E1
    Loss --> D1
    Loss --> D2
```

## 4.3 Diffusion Model for Low-Dose CT Denoising

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Full-Dose CT Training Data] --> B[Forward Diffusion - Add Noise T Steps]
    B --> C[Noisy CT at Step T]
    C --> D[U-Net Noise Predictor]
    D --> E[Predicted Noise]
    E --> F[Reverse Denoising Loop]
    F --> G[Denoised Low-Dose CT Output]
    H[Low-Dose CT Input at Inference] --> F
```

## 4.4 Clinical LLM Pipeline — Radiology Report Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chest X-ray Image] --> B[Vision Encoder - ViT or CNN]
    B --> C[Visual Token Embeddings]
    C --> D[Cross-Modal Fusion Layer]
    E[Clinical Context Prompts] --> D
    D --> F[Medical Transformer Decoder]
    F --> G[Generated Radiology Report]
    G --> H[Post-Processing & Fact-Checking]
    H --> I[Clinician Review Dashboard]
```

## 4.5 Drug Discovery Pipeline (Generative Molecular Design)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target Protein 3D Structure] --> B[Binding Site Identification]
    B --> C[Generative Model - RL + Transformer]
    C --> D[Novel SMILES Candidate Molecules]
    D --> E[Property Predictor - ADMET]
    E --> F{Valid Drug Candidate?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Wet Lab Synthesis]
    F -- No --> C
    G --> H[Clinical Trials Phase 1]
    H --> I[FDA Approval Pathway]
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Synthetic EHR Generation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real EHR Database - De-identified] --> B[Statistical Profiling Module]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction - Demographics, Diagnoses, Labs]
    C --> D[Conditional VAE Training Engine]
    D --> E[Latent Patient Representation Space]
    E --> F[Sampling Layer]
    F --> G[Synthetic Patient Record Generator]
    G --> H[Privacy Audit Module - Membership Inference Attack Test]
    H --> I{Privacy Threshold Met?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Release Synthetic Cohort]
    I -- No --> F
```

## 4.7 Multi-Modal Healthcare AI Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Clinical Need Identified] --> Q1{Data Modality?}
    Q1 -- Imaging --> IMG[Medical Image AI Track]
    Q1 -- Text --> TXT[Clinical NLP Track]
    Q1 -- Molecules --> MOL[Drug Discovery Track]
    Q1 -- Signals --> SIG[Bio-signal AI Track]
    IMG --> I1[GAN / Diffusion Pipeline]
    TXT --> T1[Transformer LLM Pipeline]
    MOL --> M1[Graph NN + RL Pipeline]
    SIG --> S1[1D CNN + Diffusion Pipeline]
    I1 --> OUT[Clinical Validation and Deployment]
    T1 --> OUT
    M1 --> OUT
    S1 --> OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`
**Define Generative AI in healthcare and list any two medical data modalities where it is applied.**

**Model Answer:**
Generative AI in healthcare refers to the use of deep generative models (GANs, VAEs, Diffusion Models, LLMs) to synthesize, augment, or interpret biomedical data such as medical images, clinical text, and molecular structures. Two key medical data modalities are:
1. **Medical Imaging** — X-ray, CT, MRI, PET scans used for diagnosis and treatment planning.
2. **Electronic Health Records (EHR)** — Structured and unstructured patient data used for clinical decision support.

*Examiner Note:* Full 2 marks require both definition *and* two modalities. A definition alone fetches **1 mark**.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**What is the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and why is it preferred over Inception Score (IS) for medical image evaluation?**

**Model Answer:**
The Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) measures the distance between feature distributions of real and generated medical images in the Inception-v3 feature space. It is computed as:

$$
\text{FID} = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)
$$

**FID is preferred over IS for medical imaging** because:
1. FID detects **intra-class mode collapse** — a critical failure mode when a GAN generates only one type of tumor.
2. FID uses *true* feature statistics (mean & covariance), not just sample-level probabilities.
3. FID is more sensitive to subtle anatomical distortions that affect clinical diagnostic accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Explain the architecture and training procedure of a **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** for synthetic medical image generation. Derive the ELBO loss function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how VAEs can be applied for **tumor anomaly detection** in brain MRI scans. Compare VAEs with GANs in this context. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
A VAE consists of:
- **Encoder $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:** Maps input medical image $x$ to a latent distribution parameterized by mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
- **Latent Space $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$:** Compressed probabilistic representation.
- **Decoder $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$:** Reconstructs the medical image from sampled latent code.

**Training Procedure (2 marks):**
The VAE is trained by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))
$$

The first term is the **reconstruction loss** (e.g., binary cross-entropy or MSE), and the second term is the **KL divergence** regularization ensuring the latent space approximates a standard normal prior. Training uses **stochastic gradient descent** with the **reparameterization trick**: $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Starting from $\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p(z) dz$, we introduce $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\right]
$$

Applying Jensen's inequality for the concave $\log$ function:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\left[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\right] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))
$$

The RHS is the ELBO — a *tractable lower bound* optimized during training.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**VAE-based Tumor Anomaly Detection (4 marks):**
A VAE is trained *only on healthy* brain MRI scans. During inference:
- The VAE reconstructs the input image from its latent representation.
- **Tumor regions** are reconstructed poorly (since they were never seen during training).
- The **reconstruction error map** highlights anomalous regions — pixels with high reconstruction error are flagged as potential tumors.
- A thresholded error map serves as a **lesion segmentation** without requiring pixel-level tumor annotations.

**Comparison with GANs (3 marks):**

| Aspect | VAE | GAN |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Training Stability** | Stable, principled (ELBO) | Unstable, mode collapse risk |
| **Sample Sharpness** | Blurry reconstructions | Sharper, more realistic images |
| **Anomaly Score** | Direct reconstruction error | Requires discriminator confidence |
| **Probabilistic Latent Space** | Yes — supports sampling | No — implicit latent distribution |
| **Use in Medical Imaging** | Better for anomaly detection | Better for high-fidelity synthesis |

GANs produce sharper images but VAEs are more reliable for *out-of-distribution* detection — critical for tumor screening.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** are used for **CT-to-MRI cross-modal synthesis**. Discuss the pix2pix and CycleGAN variants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of **Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs)** for low-dose CT denoising. Derive the simplified training loss. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The pix2pix GAN for CT $\to$ MRI translation consists of:
- **Generator (U-Net):** Takes CT slice as input, outputs synthetic MRI slice.
- **Discriminator (PatchGAN):** Classifies overlapping $70 \times 70$ patches as real or fake.
- **Combined Loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L1}$, where the $L1$ loss enforces pixel-level fidelity.

**pix2pix (2 marks):**
- Requires **paired** training data (CT and MRI of same patient, aligned slice-by-slice).
- Uses **conditional GAN** framework: $G$ takes CT $x$ and produces MRI $G(x)$, conditioned on $x$.
- Loss: $\mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}[\log D(x, y)] + \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log(1 - D(x, G(x)))]$.

**CycleGAN (2 marks):**
- Works on **unpaired** data (CT and MRI from different patients).
- Uses **two generators**: $G_{CT \to MRI}$ and $G_{MRI \to CT}$.
- Enforces **cycle-consistency**: $G_{MRI \to CT}(G_{CT \to MRI}(x)) \approx x$.
- Loss: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cycle}} = \mathbb{E}_x[\Vert G_{MRI \to CT}(G_{CT \to MRI}(x)) - x \Vert_1]$.

**Clinical Utility (1 mark):**
Eliminates the need for patients to undergo both CT and MRI scans, reducing cost, scan time, and radiation exposure.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**DDPM Working Principle (3 marks):**
DDPMs consist of two Markov chains:
1. **Forward process $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$:** Gradually adds Gaussian noise over $T$ steps until $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
2. **Reverse process $p_{\theta}(x_{t-1} \mid x_t)$:** A neural network (typically U-Net) learns to denoise step-by-step, recovering a clean medical image.

For low-dose CT denoising, the model is trained on full-dose CT scans. At inference, a low-dose (noisy) CT scan is used as the starting point $x_T$, and the reverse process *iteratively denoises* it into a clean, full-dose-equivalent image.

**Derivation of Simplified Training Loss (4 marks):**

The true variational lower bound is intractable in closed form. Ho et al. showed that a simplified noise-prediction objective works equally well:

**Step 1:** Forward diffusion gives:

$$
x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

**Step 2:** The variational bound decomposes into a tractable term and KL divergences between Gaussians. The KL term simplifies when the reverse process variance is fixed.

**Step 3:** The simplified loss becomes a noise-prediction MSE:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(x_t, t) \Vert^2\right]
$$

where $\epsilon_{\theta}(x_t, t)$ is the neural network's predicted noise. This is a *weighted* denoising score matching objective and is equivalent to training a denoising autoencoder at multiple noise levels.

**Clinical Significance:** Reduces patient radiation dose by up to **$75\%$** while maintaining diagnostic image quality — a major breakthrough in radiology.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Healthcare Generative AI Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ELBO with KL Divergence:** ELBO is *not* equal to $-D_{\text{KL}}$ alone; it includes the reconstruction term. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting the Reparameterization Trick in VAEs:** Without it, gradients cannot flow through the sampling operation. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 3. **Mixing up pix2pix and CycleGAN assumptions:** pix2pix needs *paired* data; CycleGAN works with *unpaired* data. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 4. **Writing `|x|` inside markdown tables:** This breaks KTU online exam formatting. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert`.
> 5. **Omitting the Privacy and Ethics Discussion:** Healthcare questions in KTU 2024 scheme *expect* mention of HIPAA, GDPR, or ABDM. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 6. **Using Python code in theory paper:** KTU theory papers prohibit code unless explicitly asked. Use *mathematical formulation only*.
> 7. **Not specifying evaluation metrics:** Always mention FID, SSIM, Dice, BLEU, or ROUGE-L where applicable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Healthcare:**

- [ ] Healthcare Generative AI uses **GANs, VAEs, Diffusion Models, and LLMs** for medical imaging, clinical text, drug discovery, and EHR synthesis.
- [ ] **GAN objective:** $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- [ ] **VAE ELBO:** Reconstruction $-$ KL Divergence; trained via reparameterization trick.
- [ ] **Diffusion models** use forward noising $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ and learned reverse denoising $p_{\theta}(x_{t-1} \mid x_t)$.
- [ ] **Simplified DDPM loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(x_t, t) \Vert^2]$.
- [ ] **pix2pix** = paired CT-MRI translation; **CycleGAN** = unpaired translation with cycle-consistency.
- [ ] **FID** is the gold-standard metric for generative medical image quality; lower is better.
- [ ] **SSIM** preserves structural/contrast information; **PSNR** measures pixel-level fidelity.
- [ ] **Dice coefficient** is used for medical image segmentation overlap.
- [ ] **BLEU** and **ROUGE-L** evaluate generated clinical text vs. ground-truth reports.
- [ ] **Healthcare AI must comply** with HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), ABDM (India), and FDA SaMD (US).
- [ ] **Synthetic data** solves the *triple bottleneck*: data scarcity, privacy, annotation cost.
- [ ] **Drug discovery pipelines** combine Generative Models + Reinforcement Learning + Property Prediction (ADMET).
- [ ] **BioBERT, ClinicalBERT, Med-PaLM** are Transformer LLMs fine-tuned on biomedical corpora.
- [ ] **Real-world impact:** \$200–360 billion potential annual value (JPMorgan 2023 estimate).
- [ ] **KTU 2024 trend:** Questions emphasize *derivation* + *clinical application* + *ethical discussion* — not just definitions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT756 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Finance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generative AI in Finance** refers to the application of generative artificial intelligence models — including Large Language Models (LLMs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), Transformer-based architectures, and diffusion models — to autonomously create, synthesize, analyze, and forecast financial data, documents, strategies, and decision-support artifacts within banking, investment, insurance, and regulatory ecosystems.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT756 – Generative AI, Module 4)**, Finance is treated as a **vertical application domain** where generative models are deployed to solve industry-scale problems such as:

- Synthetic financial dataset generation (for stress testing and model training)
- Automated financial report summarization and generation
- Algorithmic trading signal synthesis
- Risk modeling, fraud narrative generation, and credit scoring
- Conversational banking and advisory chatbots
- Regulatory compliance (RegTech) and ESG reporting

The mathematical foundation rests on learning a probabilistic mapping $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ (or conditional $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}\vert\mathbf{x})$) over financial manifolds, where parameters $\theta$ are optimized via maximum likelihood or adversarial objectives.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Super-Smart Financial Intern"**

Imagine you hire an exceptionally fast intern at a bank. This intern can:
1. **Read** thousands of 200-page annual reports in seconds and write a 1-page summary in plain English.
2. **Imagine** (synthesize) realistic fake customer transactions that *look* exactly like real ones — useful for training fraud-detection systems without exposing private data.
3. **Predict** what the stock market *might* do tomorrow based on patterns in past behavior, and explain *why* in human language.
4. **Chat** with customers 24/7 about their loan eligibility, savings plans, or investment advice.

That intern is **Generative AI in Finance**. Instead of merely classifying inputs (e.g., "Is this transaction fraud? Yes/No"), it **generates new outputs** — text, numbers, charts, scenarios — that didn't exist before.

The key differentiator from classical AI in finance:
- **Discriminative AI** → Answers "What is this?" (classification, regression)
- **Generative AI** → Answers "What could this become?" or "Write me one like this" (generation, synthesis, simulation)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Generative AI for Finance

Key performance indicators used to benchmark generative systems in financial contexts:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Target |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| Inception Score | $IS$ | $> 8$ |
| Fréchet Inception Distance | $FID$ | $< 50$ |
| Perplexity (LLM) | $PPL$ | $< 20$ |
| BLEU / ROUGE-L (text) | — | $> 0.4$ |
| Sharpe Ratio uplift | $\Delta S_r$ | $> 0.5$ |
| Annual Percentage Rate (cost basis) | $APR$ | varies |
| Value at Risk | $VaR_{\alpha}$ | depends on $\alpha$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Sharpe Ratio** is a cornerstone finance metric defined as:
> $$S_r = \frac{\mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f]}{\sigma_p}$$
> where $R_p$ is portfolio return, $R_f$ is the risk-free rate, and $\sigma_p$ is the standard deviation of excess returns. Generative AI systems are often evaluated by how much they *improve* this metric relative to baseline strategies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Architecture of Generative AI in Finance

Generative AI for finance operates through three core pillars, each grounded in distinct mathematical machinery.

### Pillar 1: Generative Pre-trained Transformers (GPT-style LLMs)
LLMs are autoregressive language models that factorize a joint probability distribution over a sequence of tokens. For a financial document $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$:

$$p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p_{\theta}(x_t \vert x_{<t})$$

The model is trained by **minimizing the negative log-likelihood** (cross-entropy loss):

$$\mathcal{L}_{LLM}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p_{\theta}(x_t \vert x_{<t})$$

**Finance Applications:**
- Earnings call transcript summarization
- Regulatory filing (10-K, 10-Q) parsing
- Automated financial advisory chatbots

### Pillar 2: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
GANs consist of a Generator $G$ and a Discriminator $D$ locked in a min-max game:

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$

**Finance Applications:**
- Synthetic credit card transaction generation
- Synthetic market data for stress testing
- Fraud pattern augmentation

### Pillar 3: Diffusion Models
Diffusion models learn to reverse a noise-injection process. The forward process adds Gaussian noise over $T$ steps:

$$q(\mathbf{x}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \beta_t \mathbf{I})$$

The reverse process learns $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \vert \mathbf{x}_t)$ to denoise.

**Finance Applications:**
- Scenario generation for Monte Carlo risk simulations
- Synthetic time-series for option pricing

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reasoning |
|------|-----------|
| Tokenization of financial text | LLMs require discrete units; sub-word tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) handles jargon like "EBITDA", "VaR" |
| Fine-tuning on financial corpora | Pre-trained models lack domain knowledge; fine-tuning on SEC filings, Bloomberg data injects finance-specific semantics |
| RLHF for advisory systems | Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback aligns outputs with fiduciary duty and regulatory compliance |
| Synthetic data via GANs | Solves the **data scarcity** problem in fraud detection (real fraud is < 0.1% of transactions) |
| Stress scenario synthesis | Diffusion models generate tail-risk events not present in historical data |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Bank — Memorize These for KTU ESE**

| # | Concept | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | Autoregressive generation | $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p_{\theta}(x_t \vert x_{<t})$ | LLM text generation |
| 2 | Cross-entropy loss | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Training LLMs |
| 3 | GAN objective | $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$ | Synthetic transactions |
| 4 | Diffusion forward | $q(\mathbf{x}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{1-\beta_t}\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \beta_t \mathbf{I})$ | Noising process |
| 5 | Diffusion reverse | $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}\vert\mathbf{x}_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_t, t), \Sigma_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_t, t))$ | Denoising process |
| 6 | VAE ELBO | $\mathcal{L}_{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{z})] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}\vert\mathbf{x}) \Vert p(\mathbf{z}))$ | Latent financial features |
| 7 | Sharpe Ratio | $S_r = \frac{\mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f]}{\sigma_p}$ | Strategy evaluation |
| 8 | Value at Risk | $VaR_{\alpha} = -\inf\{x : P(L > x) \leq 1-\alpha\}$ | Risk modeling |
| 9 | FID Score | $FID = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2})$ | Synthetic data quality |
| 10 | Black-Scholes (call) | $C = S_0 N(d_1) - K e^{-rT} N(d_2)$ | Option pricing baseline |
| 11 | Attention mechanism | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Transformer backbone |
| 12 | KL Divergence | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_i P(i) \log \frac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | Distribution matching |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

Generative AI in finance powers mission-critical systems at scale:

- **JPMorgan Chase** — COiN (Contract Intelligence) platform reviews 12,000 commercial credit agreements in seconds using NLP.
- **Goldman Sachs** — Uses LLM-based assistants for IPO prospectus drafting.
- **BloombergGPT** — 50B parameter LLM fine-tuned specifically on financial text.
- **Mastercard** — Deploys GANs to synthesize fraudulent transaction patterns for training detection models.
- **Two Sigma / Citadel** — Diffusion-based scenario generation for options and derivatives pricing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Training Objective of a Financial LLM via Cross-Entropy Minimization

**Problem Statement:** Given a corpus of financial documents $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{(N)}\}$, derive the loss function used to fine-tune a pre-trained LLM for financial summarization.

**Step 1 — Define the autoregressive factorization.**
For a sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$:

$$p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p_{\theta}(x_t \vert x_{<t})$$

This expresses joint probability as a product of conditionals.

**Step 2 — Apply the log transformation.**
Take the natural logarithm to convert products into sums:

$$\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \log \prod_{t=1}^{T} p_{\theta}(x_t \vert x_{<t}) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log p_{\theta}(x_t \vert x_{<t})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the log-likelihood across the entire corpus.**
Sum over all $N$ documents in dataset $\mathcal{D}$:

$$\log p_{\theta}(\mathcal{D}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_n} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(x_t^{(n)} \Big\vert x_{<t}^{(n)}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert maximization to minimization.**
Training aims to maximize likelihood; we negate to form a loss:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\log p_{\theta}(\mathcal{D}) = -\sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_n} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(x_t^{(n)} \Big\vert x_{<t}^{(n)}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Normalize by total token count for stable optimization.**
Let $M = \sum_{n=1}^{N} T_n$ be the total token count:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{M} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_n} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(x_t^{(n)} \Big\vert x_{<t}^{(n)}\right)$$

This is the **average cross-entropy loss per token**.

**Step 6 — Express as cross-entropy with one-hot labels.**
For token $x_t$ with one-hot vector $\mathbf{y}_t$, and predicted distribution $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{M} \sum_{t=1}^{M} \sum_{v \in \mathcal{V}} y_{t,v} \log \hat{y}_{t,v}$$

**Step 7 — Perplexity as evaluation metric.**
Perplexity is the exponentiated cross-entropy:

$$PPL = \exp(\mathcal{L}_{CE})$$

Lower perplexity indicates better language modeling performance — a critical benchmark for finance-tuned LLMs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: GAN for Synthetic Financial Transaction Generation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the optimal discriminator for a GAN trained to synthesize fake credit card transactions that mimic a real distribution $p_{\text{data}}$.

**Step 1 — State the value function.**

$$V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Step 2 — Fix G and optimize D.**
For any fixed generator $G$, the optimal discriminator $D^*(x)$ satisfies:

$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

**Proof (sketch):**
The objective for $D$ is:

$$\int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx$$

Taking derivative with respect to $D(x)$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0$$

Solving:

$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back to get the GAN value at optimum.**

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot D_{JS}(p_{\text{data}} \Vert p_g)$$

where $D_{JS}$ is the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence**:

$$D_{JS}(P \Vert Q) = \frac{1}{2} D_{KL}(P \Vert M) + \frac{1}{2} D_{KL}(Q \Vert M)$$

with $M = \frac{P+Q}{2}$.

**Step 4 — Global minimum occurs when $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.**
When $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, then $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$, and $V = -\log 4$. The JS divergence vanishes.

**Step 5 — Practical training: non-saturating loss.**
To avoid vanishing gradients early in training, use:

$$\mathcal{L}_G = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log D(G(z))]$$

instead of $\min \log(1 - D(G(z)))$. This is the standard non-saturating GAN loss used in finance-grade synthetic data tools like **SDV (Synthetic Data Vault)** and **YData**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Value at Risk (VaR) Computation via Diffusion-Generated Scenarios

**Problem Statement:** Use a diffusion model to generate $N$ synthetic market scenarios and estimate $VaR_{\alpha}$ for a portfolio.

**Step 1 — Define VaR formally.**

$$VaR_{\alpha} = -\inf\{x \in \mathbb{R} : P(L \leq x) \geq \alpha\}$$

where $L$ is the portfolio loss and $\alpha = 0.99$ (99% confidence) is standard.

**Step 2 — Generate synthetic returns via diffusion sampling.**
Start from $\mathbf{x}_T \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ and run the learned reverse process for $T$ steps to obtain synthetic return vectors $r^{(i)}$.

**Step 3 — Compute portfolio losses.**
For each synthetic scenario $i$, compute the portfolio loss:

$$L^{(i)} = -\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{r}^{(i)} \cdot V_0$$

where $\mathbf{w}$ is the weight vector and $V_0$ is initial portfolio value.

**Step 4 — Empirical quantile estimate.**
Sort losses $L^{(1)}, L^{(2)}, \ldots, L^{(N)}$ in ascending order. The empirical $VaR_{\alpha}$ is the $\lceil \alpha N \rceil$-th smallest value:

$$\widehat{VaR}_{\alpha} = L_{(\lceil \alpha N \rceil)}$$

**Step 5 — Conditional VaR (Expected Shortfall) for tail-risk analysis.**

$$CVaR_{\alpha} = \mathbb{E}[L \vert L \geq VaR_{\alpha}] = \frac{1}{N(1-\alpha)} \sum_{i: L^{(i)} \geq VaR_{\alpha}} L^{(i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why diffusion for VaR?** Historical VaR only sees past crises (e.g., 2008, 2020). Diffusion models can synthesize **plausible but unobserved** tail events — critical for Basel III/IV regulatory stress testing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Survey of Generative Models in Finance

| Model Family | Core Mechanism | Strength in Finance | Limitation |
|--------------|----------------|---------------------|------------|
| **LLM (GPT)** | Autoregressive transformer | Document summarization, Q&A, advisory | Hallucination, no native numeracy |
| **GAN** | Adversarial min-max | High-fidelity synthetic tabular data | Mode collapse, unstable training |
| **VAE** | Latent variable, ELBO | Smooth interpolation in feature space | Blurry outputs |
| **Diffusion** | Iterative denoising | Scenario generation, time-series synthesis | Slow inference (many steps) |
| **Transformer-Encoder (BERT-style)** | Masked language modeling | Sentiment analysis, named entity recognition (NER) on filings | Not generative (discriminative) |
| **Mixture of Experts (MoE)** | Sparse routing | Domain-specific financial sub-tasks | High compute footprint |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Architecture: Generative AI Pipeline for Finance

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the end-to-end pipeline of a production-grade Generative AI system deployed in a financial institution.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Financial Data Sources] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C1[SEC Filings and 10-K Reports]
    B --> C2[Market Tick Data]
    B --> C3[Customer Transaction Logs]
    B --> C4[News and Earnings Calls]
    C1 --> D[Preprocessing and Tokenization]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Feature Engineering and Embedding]
    E --> F1[LLM Module for Text Tasks]
    E --> F2[GAN Module for Synthetic Data]
    E --> F3[Diffusion Module for Scenarios]
    F1 --> G1[Summarization Engine]
    F1 --> G2[Advisory Chatbot]
    F2 --> G3[Fraud Training Data Generator]
    F3 --> G4[Stress Scenario Generator]
    G1 --> H[Compliance and Risk Dashboard]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I1[Regulator Reporting]
    H --> I2[Risk Officer Decision Support]
    H --> I3[Customer Facing Interface]
    I1 --> J[Audit and Governance Layer]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

## 4.2 Use-Case Mapping Matrix (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Historical Prices]
        I2[Transaction Records]
        I3[Regulatory Texts]
        I4[Customer Queries]
    end
    subgraph GenerativeAI[Generative AI Engine]
        M1[Transformer LLM]
        M2[GAN Network]
        M3[Diffusion Model]
        M4[VAE Encoder-Decoder]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Financial Reports]
        O2[Synthetic Transactions]
        O3[Risk Scenarios]
        O4[Chatbot Responses]
    end
    I1 --> M3
    I2 --> M2
    I3 --> M1
    I4 --> M1
    M1 --> O1
    M1 --> O4
    M2 --> O2
    M3 --> O3
    M4 --> O2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Generative Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Financial Task Identified] --> B{Output Modality?}
    B -->|Text| C{Document Length?}
    B -->|Tabular or Numeric| D{Data Volume?}
    B -->|Time Series| E{Use Case?}
    C -->|Long Documents| F[Use GPT Style LLM with Long Context]
    C -->|Short Queries| G[Use Fine Tuned BERT or T5]
    D -->|Small Real Samples| H[Use GAN for Augmentation]
    D -->|Large Real Samples| I[Use VAE for Latent Features]
    E -->|Risk Simulation| J[Use Diffusion Model]
    E -->|Trading Signal| K[Use Transformer with RLHF]
    F --> L[Deploy and Monitor]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.4 RLHF Loop for Financial Advisory LLMs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Query about Investment] --> B[LLM Generates Response]
    B --> C[Human Finance Expert Ranks Outputs]
    C --> D[Reward Model Trained on Rankings]
    D --> E[Policy Updated via PPO]
    E --> B
    E --> F[Compliance Filter Layer]
    F --> G[Final Advisory Output to User]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Reasoning:** Since direct physical drawings of LLMs/GANs are not meaningful, the diagrams above represent the *functional architecture* of how data flows through a generative finance pipeline — which is exactly how the KTU 2024 OBE framework expects students to map AI systems.
