# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Generative AI in the context of finance. Give two real-world use cases.**

**Model Answer:**

Generative AI in finance refers to the use of models such as Large Language Models (LLMs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), and Diffusion Models to **autonomously create, synthesize, or summarize** financial data, text, or scenarios, rather than merely classifying or predicting from existing inputs.

**Two Real-World Use Cases:**
1. **Automated Regulatory Report Generation:** LLMs fine-tuned on SEC filings (e.g., BloombergGPT) can automatically draft earnings summaries and compliance reports, reducing analyst workload by up to **80%**.
2. **Synthetic Fraud Data Generation:** GANs are used by payment networks like Mastercard to generate realistic fraudulent transaction patterns, augmenting rare-class training data for fraud-detection classifiers.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between discriminative and generative models. Why is generative modeling preferred for synthetic financial data?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Discriminative Model | Generative Model |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Objective** | Learn $P(y \vert x)$ (decision boundary) | Learn $P(x)$ or $P(x \vert y)$ (data distribution) |
| **Task** | Classification, regression | Synthesis, generation, simulation |
| **Example** | Logistic Regression, BERT | GPT, GAN, VAE, Diffusion |

**Why generative modeling is preferred for synthetic financial data:**
1. **Data scarcity:** Real fraud cases are < 0.1% of transactions. Generative models like GANs can synthesize balanced datasets to train robust fraud detectors.
2. **Privacy compliance:** Synthetic data avoids leaking Personally Identifiable Information (PII), satisfying GDPR and RBI data-localization norms.
3. **Stress testing:** Diffusion models generate *plausible extreme scenarios* not present in historical data — critical for Basel III capital adequacy requirements.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture and working of a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). Derive its loss function and discuss how it can be applied to generate synthetic credit card transactions for fraud detection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, describe the application of Large Language Models (LLMs) in financial document summarization. Discuss the role of fine-tuning, prompt engineering, and RLHF in ensuring regulatory compliance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) GAN Architecture and Synthetic Transaction Generation:**

**Architecture:**
A GAN comprises two neural networks trained simultaneously:
- **Generator (G):** Maps random noise $\mathbf{z} \sim p_z$ (typically $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$) to synthetic transaction data $\mathbf{x}_{\text{fake}} = G(\mathbf{z})$.
- **Discriminator (D):** A binary classifier distinguishing real transactions $\mathbf{x}_{\text{real}}$ from fake ones, outputting $D(\mathbf{x}) \in [0, 1]$.

**Value Function Derivation:**

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$

**Optimal Discriminator** (derived in Section 3.2):

$$D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Application to Fraud Detection:**
1. **Problem:** Real fraud transactions are extremely rare (~0.01% of total). Classifiers trained on imbalanced data suffer high false-negative rates.
2. **Solution:** Train a GAN on legitimate transactions, then use the generator $G$ to produce synthetic fraudulent-looking samples (by injecting fraud patterns into the latent space).
3. **Augmentation:** Combine synthetic fraud samples with real data to balance the training set.
4. **Evaluation:** Measure synthetic-data fidelity using **FID score** and downstream classifier AUC improvement.

**(b) LLMs for Financial Document Summarization:**

**Architecture:** Transformer decoder stack (e.g., GPT-3.5, BloombergGPT, FinGPT) with self-attention:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

**Workflow:**
1. **Ingestion:** Load SEC 10-K filings, earnings transcripts, or annual reports.
2. **Chunking & Embedding:** Split documents into 512–4096 token chunks; embed via the LLM.
3. **Prompt Engineering:** Use structured prompts like *"Summarize the following 10-K filing in 5 bullet points, focusing on revenue growth, risk factors, and forward-looking statements."*
4. **Generation:** LLM produces a summary autoregressively.
5. **Post-processing:** Apply rule-based filters for numerical consistency (no hallucinated figures).

**Role of Fine-Tuning, Prompting, and RLHF:**

| Technique | Purpose in Finance |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **Domain Fine-Tuning** | Inject finance-specific vocabulary (EBITDA, VaR, CDS) into the model |
| **Instruction Tuning** | Train on (instruction, financial response) pairs |
| **RLHF** | Align outputs with fiduciary duty; reject risky advice |
| **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** | Ground responses in real-time SEC filings to prevent hallucination |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) failing to derive the optimal discriminator equation, (2) confusing GAN with VAE objectives, (3) omitting the role of **RAG** in compliance, and (4) not mentioning hallucination as a key LLM risk in finance. Always draw a labeled diagram for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the forward and reverse diffusion processes. Derive the training objective of a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM). Discuss its application in synthetic market scenario generation for Value-at-Risk (VaR) estimation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the ethical, regulatory, and operational risks of deploying Generative AI in banking and financial services. Suggest a governance framework for responsible deployment. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Diffusion Processes and Market Scenario Generation:**

**Forward Process (Noise Injection):**
Given clean market data $\mathbf{x}_0$ (e.g., daily returns), gradually add Gaussian noise over $T$ steps:

$$q(\mathbf{x}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \beta_t \mathbf{I})$$

where $\beta_t \in (0, 1)$ is a variance schedule (linear or cosine).

**Closed-form marginal:**

$$q(\mathbf{x}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t; \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{x}_0, (1-\bar{\alpha}_t)\mathbf{I})$$

with $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}(1-\beta_s)$.

**Reverse Process (Learned Denoising):**
Train a neural network $\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t)$ to predict the noise added at each step:

$$p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \vert \mathbf{x}_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}; \mu_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_t, t), \Sigma_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_t, t))$$

**Training Objective (Simplified):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, \mathbf{x}_0, \epsilon}\!\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t) \Vert^2\right]$$

where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ and $\mathbf{x}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{x}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\epsilon$.

**Application to VaR Estimation:**
1. Train a diffusion model on historical S&P 500 returns.
2. Sample 10,000 synthetic return paths from the trained model.
3. Compute portfolio losses $L^{(i)} = -\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{r}^{(i)} V_0$.
4. Estimate VaR at 99% confidence:

$$\widehat{VaR}_{0.99} = L_{(\lceil 0.99 \times 10000 \rceil)}$$

5. Estimate Conditional VaR (Expected Shortfall) for tail-risk reporting.

**(b) Ethical, Regulatory, and Operational Risks:**

| Risk Category | Description | Mitigation |
|---------------|-------------|------------|
| **Hallucination** | LLM invents fictitious financial figures | RAG, human-in-the-loop, fact-checking layer |
| **Bias & Fairness** | Model denies loans to protected groups | Adversarial debiasing, fairness audits |
| **Data Privacy** | Memorization of customer PII | Differential privacy, federated learning |
| **Model Risk (SR 11-7)** | Black-box decisions violate explainability | SHAP, LIME, attention visualization |
| **Systemic Risk** | Many firms using similar GenAI herding | Regulatory diversification mandates |
| **Regulatory Non-Compliance** | Outputs violate GDPR, RBI, SEBI rules | Compliance filter layer, audit trails |

**Proposed Governance Framework:**

1. **Layer 1 — Model Risk Management:** Adopt Federal Reserve's **SR 11-7** guidelines for model validation, ongoing monitoring, and independent review.
2. **Layer 2 — Data Governance:** Enforce data provenance, lineage tracking, and consent management.
3. **Layer 3 — Explainability:** Mandate post-hoc explanation (SHAP values) for every customer-facing AI decision.
4. **Layer 4 — Human-in-the-Loop:** Require human sign-off for high-stakes decisions (loan approvals > ₹10 lakh).
5. **Layer 5 — Continuous Monitoring:** Real-time drift detection, hallucination rate tracking, fairness metrics.
6. **Layer 6 — Audit & Compliance:** Quarterly audits by internal risk teams; annual external audit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often (1) skip the derivation of the diffusion training loss, (2) confuse forward/reverse process notations, (3) fail to link VaR estimation concretely to diffusion samples, (4) treat ethics superficially without naming specific frameworks (SR 11-7, GDPR, EU AI Act). Always structure ethics answers with a **risk-mitigation table**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Finance**

### Core Definitions
- Generative AI in finance = models that **create/synthesize** financial text, data, or scenarios
- Discriminative vs Generative: $P(y \vert x)$ vs $P(x)$ or $P(x \vert y)$

### Must-Know Models
- **LLM (GPT)** → Text summarization, advisory chatbots
- **GAN** → Synthetic transactions, fraud data augmentation
- **VAE** → Latent feature learning, anomaly detection
- **Diffusion (DDPM)** → Scenario generation, VaR stress testing
- **Transformer** → Backbone of all above (self-attention mechanism)

### Must-Know Formulas (Examiner's Favorite)
1. Autoregressive factorization: $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p_{\theta}(x_t \vert x_{<t})$
2. Cross-entropy loss: $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum y_i \log \hat{y}_i$
3. GAN min-max: $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$
4. Optimal discriminator: $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}$
5. Diffusion forward: $q(\mathbf{x}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{1-\beta_t}\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \beta_t \mathbf{I})$
6. Diffusion loss: $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t) \Vert^2]$
7. Attention: $\text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k}) V$
8. Sharpe Ratio: $S_r = (\mathbb{E}[R_p] - R_f) / \sigma_p$
9. VaR: $VaR_\alpha = -\inf\{x : P(L \leq x) \geq \alpha\}$
10. KL Divergence: $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum P(i) \log(P(i)/Q(i))$

### Key Industry Examples
- **BloombergGPT** — 50B parameter finance LLM
- **JPMorgan COiN** — Contract analysis
- **Mastercard** — GAN-based fraud simulation
- **FinGPT** — Open-source finance LLM

### Critical Risk Topics (Always Appear in Exam)
- Hallucination in LLMs → RAG + human-in-the-loop
- Bias in credit scoring → Adversarial debiasing
- Model risk → SR 11-7 compliance
- Privacy → Differential privacy
- Systemic risk from model herding

### Exam Strategy Tips
1. Always draw a **labeled architecture diagram** for any model (GAN, LLM, Diffusion) — earns 2–3 easy marks.
2. Always **derive** the loss function step-by-step — partial credit even if final answer is wrong.
3. Always include **one real-world example** (Bloomberg, JPMorgan, Mastercard) — examiners reward industry context.
4. For ethics questions, use a **risk-mitigation table** — clean, scannable, full marks.
5. Memorize **FID, Sharpe Ratio, VaR, Perplexity** — they appear in almost every KTU paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-world Use Cases and Challenges in Deploying Generative AI Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT756 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-world Use Cases and Challenges in Deploying Generative AI Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Real-world deployment of Generative AI** refers to the operationalization of foundation models (Large Language Models, Diffusion Models, GANs, etc.) into production-grade engineering pipelines that serve end-users with non-functional guarantees on **latency, throughput, cost, safety, and reliability**. Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic maps to **Course Outcomes (COs)** involving the analysis of socio-technical trade-offs in AI system design, ethical risk auditing, and the engineering of scalable AI services.

$$ \text{GenAI Deployment} = f(\text{Model}, \text{Infrastructure}, \text{Data Pipeline}, \text{Safety Layer}, \text{Evaluation Loop}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** The term "deployment" in the context of Generative AI is **not** equivalent to "model training." Deployment is the *serving* phase where a trained model is integrated into a product surface (chatbot, IDE plugin, search engine, ERP, etc.) and is exposed to adversarial, diverse, and high-volume real-world traffic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Analogy:** Imagine you have perfected a recipe (the trained GenAI model) in your home kitchen. *Real-world deployment* is the act of opening that recipe as a **chain restaurant** that must serve **10,000 customers per hour**, across **multiple cities**, while maintaining **consistent taste**, **low wait time**, **food safety (FDA) compliance**, and **low cost per plate**. The recipe itself (the model) is only 20% of the problem — the *kitchen layout, supply chain, staff training, hygiene audits, billing system, and customer feedback loop* constitute the remaining 80%, which is precisely what this module addresses.

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Production GenAI

A KTU-graded response must identify and explain the **five engineering pillars** that govern every real-world deployment:

1. **Pillar I – Functional Correctness:** Does the model produce factually aligned, instruction-following outputs?
2. **Pillar II – Non-Functional Performance:** Latency (Time To First Token, TTFT), Throughput (tokens/sec), Availability SLA (e.g., **99.9%**).
3. **Pillar III – Economic Viability:** Cost per 1K tokens, GPU-hours, and Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).
4. **Pillar IV – Trust, Safety & Compliance:** Bias mitigation, hallucination control, GDPR / DPDP Act / EU AI Act adherence.
5. **Pillar V – Observability & Evals:** Continuous benchmarking using metrics such as BLEU, ROUGE, FID, BERTScore, and human-in-the-loop review.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Deployed GenAI System

A GenAI service in production is rarely a single model. It is a **compound AI system**. The following structured breakdown follows the canonical architecture taught in KTU Module 4:

- **Step 1 — Ingress / API Gateway:** Receives user prompt, performs authentication, rate-limiting, and request normalization.
- **Step 2 — Pre-Processing & Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG):** Query rewriting, embedding generation, vector database lookup (e.g., FAISS, Pinecone), and prompt augmentation with retrieved context.
- **Step 3 — Inference Engine:** The LLM/Diffusion model executes forward passes. Modern engines use techniques like *PagedAttention* (vLLM), *KV-cache quantization*, and *speculative decoding*.
- **Step 4 — Post-Processing:** Output parsing, content moderation (safety classifiers), citation verification, and structured output validation (JSON-schema enforcement).
- **Step 5 — Egress & Telemetry:** Streaming response via Server-Sent Events (SSE) / WebSockets, with asynchronous logging to observability stacks (Langfuse, Phoenix, Weights & Biases).
- **Step 6 — Feedback Loop:** User thumbs-up/down, RLHF signals, and offline evals feed the next fine-tuning cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why behind RAG:** Pure parametric LLMs hallucinate because their knowledge is frozen at training time and stored only in weights. RAG externalizes knowledge to a **retrievable vector store**, reducing hallucination by an estimated **30–60%** in enterprise benchmarks without retraining the base model.

## 2.2 Major Real-World Use Cases (Industry-Verified)

The following table consolidates the deployment domains that KTU examiners repeatedly test:

| **Use Case Domain** | **Generative Model Family** | **Production Maturity** | **Key Engineering Challenge** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conversational Customer Support (e.g., banking chatbots) | Decoder-only LLMs (Transformer) | **High** | Tone consistency, PII redaction |
| Code Generation (GitHub Copilot, Cursor) | Code-LLMs (Code Llama, StarCoder) | **High** | License compliance, security exploits |
| Drug Discovery & Molecular Design | Graph Diffusion + LLMs | **Medium** | Wet-lab validation loop, hallucinated molecules |
| Marketing Copy & SEO Content | Instruction-tuned LLMs | **High** | Brand voice drift, SEO cannibalization |
| Image Synthesis for E-commerce | Latent Diffusion (Stable Diffusion XL, SD3) | **High** | GPU cost, brand IP leakage |
| Synthetic Data Generation for Tabular/Healthcare | GANs, VAEs, TabDDPM | **Medium-High** | Membership inference leakage |
| Voice & Music Generation (ElevenLabs, Suno) | Audio Diffusion + Codec LLMs | **Medium** | Latency, deepfake regulations |
| Enterprise Search & Document Q&A | RAG + Rerankers | **Very High** | Chunking strategy, hallucinated citations |
| Robotics & Embodied AI | Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models | **Low–Medium** | Sim-to-real gap, safety |
| Personalized Education Tutors | Fine-tuned LLMs + memory | **Medium** | Pedagogical accuracy, child-safety (COPPA/Kerala State Guidelines) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Equations for Module 4** — Memorize the boxed units and constants.

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{\text{total}}$ | Total inference cost | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{GPU}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{network}} + C_{\text{energy}}$ | USD / month |
| $C_{\text{tok}}$ | Cost per 1K tokens | $C_{\text{tok}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{GPU-hours}}}{T \cdot 1000}$ | USD / 1K tokens |
| $T_{\text{TTFT}}$ | Time To First Token | $T_{\text{TTFT}} = t_{\text{queue}} + t_{\text{prefill}}$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| $T_{\text{E2E}}$ | End-to-end latency | $T_{\text{E2E}} = t_{\text{queue}} + t_{\text{prefill}} + n \cdot t_{\text{decode}}$ | seconds (s) |
| $\eta_{\text{thr}}$ | Throughput | $\eta_{\text{thr}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{tokens}}}{t_{\text{batch}}}$ | tokens / second |
| $P_{\text{eGPU}}$ | Energy per token | $P_{\text{eGPU}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{GPU}} \cdot P_{\text{TDP}}}{N_{\text{tokens}}}$ | Joules / token |
| $R_{\text{KV}}$ | KV-cache memory | $R_{\text{KV}} = 2 \cdot n_{\text{layers}} \cdot n_{\text{heads}} \cdot d_{\text{head}} \cdot s \cdot p$ | bytes |
| $F_{\text{FID}}$ | Fréchet Inception Distance | $F_{\text{FID}} = \vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert^2 + \text{Tr}\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | scalar (lower is better) |
| $H_{\text{rate}}$ | Hallucination rate | $H_{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{hallucinated}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | ratio (0 to 1) |

**Where the symbols denote:**
- $n_{\text{layers}}$ = number of Transformer decoder layers
- $s$ = sequence length in tokens
- $p$ = precision in bytes (2 for FP16, 1 for INT8)
- $\mu_r, \mu_g$ = mean feature activations (real vs. generated) for FID
- $\Sigma_r, \Sigma_g$ = covariance matrices for FID

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises. They directly drive engineering decisions at companies like **OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and Infosys Topaz**. For example, the $C_{\text{tok}}$ metric determines whether a chatbot product can be priced profitably at **\$0.002/1K tokens** for consumers. The KV-cache equation $R_{\text{KV}}$ dictates whether a 70B parameter model can fit in a single **80 GB H100 GPU** or requires tensor parallelism across 4 GPUs — a multi-thousand-dollar infrastructure decision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — End-to-End Latency Decomposition

This derivation is critical for **KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical questions**. We derive the formula for end-to-end latency from first principles.

**Given:**
- A user prompt of length $s_{\text{in}}$ tokens.
- A generated response of length $s_{\text{out}}$ tokens.
- The LLM uses an autoregressive decoder with KV-cache reuse.

**Step 1 — Identify the three latency components of an autoregressive LLM call.**

$$ t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{queue}} + t_{\text{prefill}} + t_{\text{decode}} $$

**Step 2 — Express $t_{\text{prefill}}$ (parallel processing of input prompt).**
The prefill stage processes all $s_{\text{in}}$ tokens in parallel using matrix-matrix multiplication. Time is proportional to prompt length and quadratic in hidden dimension $d$.

$$ t_{\text{prefill}} = k_1 \cdot s_{\text{in}} \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{\text{ops}}^{-1} $$

where $p_{\text{ops}}$ is the peak FLOPs of the GPU (e.g., **989 TFLOPs** for H100 FP8) and $k_1$ is a hardware efficiency constant (~2 to 4).

**Step 3 — Express $t_{\text{decode}}$ (sequential token generation).**
Each new token is generated one at a time, but reuses the KV-cache. Time grows linearly with output length.

$$ t_{\text{decode}} = s_{\text{out}} \cdot t_{\text{per-token}} $$

$$ t_{\text{per-token}} = k_2 \cdot s_{\text{curr}} \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{\text{ops}}^{-1} $$

where $s_{\text{curr}}$ is the *current* context length, which grows from $s_{\text{in}}$ to $s_{\text{in}} + s_{\text{out}}$ during generation.

**Step 4 — Approximate $s_{\text{curr}}$ with the average context length.**
For tractability in board examinations, KTU expects the assumption $s_{\text{curr}} \approx s_{\text{in}} + \dfrac{s_{\text{out}}}{2}$.

$$ t_{\text{decode}} \approx s_{\text{out}} \cdot k_2 \cdot \left( s_{\text{in}} + \frac{s_{\text{out}}}{2} \right) \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{\text{ops}}^{-1} $$

**Step 5 — Combine all terms into the final E2E latency equation.**

$$ \boxed{\; T_{\text{E2E}} = t_{\text{queue}} + k_1 \cdot s_{\text{in}} \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{\text{ops}}^{-1} + s_{\text{out}} \cdot k_2 \cdot \left( s_{\text{in}} + \frac{s_{\text{out}}}{2} \right) \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{\text{ops}}^{-1} \;} $$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- The prefill term is **linear in input length** because modern GPUs parallelize the prompt.
- The decode term is **quadratic in output length** because each new token depends on all prior tokens through attention.
- This asymmetry is why **streaming** is essential for UX — users see the first token after $T_{\text{TTFT}}$, not after $T_{\text{E2E}}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Cost Per 1K Tokens

This derivation underpins every GenAI pricing decision in industry (e.g., GPT-4 Turbo at **\$0.01/1K input tokens**).

**Given:**
- GPU rental cost: $C_{\text{GPU}}$ = **\$2.00 per hour** for an A100 80 GB (as of 2024 industry average).
- Model hidden dimension $d = 12{,}288$ for a 70B-parameter model.
- Effective FLOPs utilization $U$ ≈ 35% of peak.

**Step 1 — Compute effective FLOPs throughput of one GPU.**

$$ p_{\text{eff}} = p_{\text{peak}} \cdot U = 989 \cdot 0.35 = 346.15 \; \text{TFLOPs} $$

**Step 2 — Compute FLOPs required to generate one token.**
For a decoder LLM, generating one new token after a context of length $s$ requires approximately $2 \cdot N_{\text{params}}$ FLOPs, where $N_{\text{params}}$ is the parameter count.

$$ f_{\text{tok}} \approx 2 \cdot N_{\text{params}} = 2 \cdot 70 \times 10^9 = 1.4 \times 10^{11} \; \text{FLOPs/token} $$

**Step 3 — Compute tokens generated per GPU-second.**

$$ \eta_{\text{thr}} = \frac{p_{\text{eff}}}{f_{\text{tok}}} = \frac{346.15 \times 10^{12}}{1.4 \times 10^{11}} \approx 2472 \; \text{tokens/sec} $$

**Step 4 — Convert tokens/sec to cost per 1K tokens.**

$$ \eta_{\text{hour}} = 2472 \cdot 3600 = 8.9 \times 10^6 \; \text{tokens/hour} $$

$$ C_{\text{tok}} = \frac{C_{\text{GPU}}}{\eta_{\text{hour}} \cdot 1000} = \frac{2.00}{8.9 \times 10^6 / 1000} $$

$$ \boxed{\; C_{\text{tok}} \approx \$2.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per 1K tokens} \;} $$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
This raw compute cost is the **floor**. Industry pricing (e.g., \$0.01/1K tokens) is **~44× higher** because it must also cover:
- Prefill cost (paid for but not counted in decode-only math)
- KV-cache memory bandwidth bottleneck
- Idle GPU time between requests
- Profit margin and R&D amortization

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Challenges in Deployment

KTU 2024 examiners reward answers that **categorize challenges into tiers**. The matrix below is the canonical structure:

| **Tier** | **Challenge** | **Engineering Mitigation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1 — Compute & Scale** | GPU scarcity, KV-cache memory blow-up, OOM at long context | vLLM PagedAttention, quantization (INT4/INT8), tensor parallelism, FlashAttention-2 |
| **Tier 2 — Quality & Trust** | Hallucination, factuality, citation fabrication | RAG, self-consistency, chain-of-verification, constrained decoding (JSON-schema, grammar) |
| **Tier 3 — Safety & Ethics** | Bias, toxicity, jailbreaks (DAN, prompt injection) | RLHF, Constitutional AI, red-teaming, content classifiers (Llama Guard, ShieldGemma) |
| **Tier 4 — Regulatory & Legal** | GDPR, DPDP Act 2023, EU AI Act, copyright (NYT v. OpenAI) | Data lineage tracking, differential privacy, on-prem deployment, license-aware RAG |
| **Tier 5 — Operational** | Eval drift, model versioning, regression detection | Online evals (Braintrust, Langfuse), shadow deployments, A/B testing, canary rollouts |
| **Tier 6 — Economic** | Unsustainable inference cost at scale | Distillation (teacher → student), LoRA fine-tuning, caching, speculative decoding |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
**Q: Differentiate between RAG-based deployment and fine-tuning-based deployment of an LLM. When would you prefer one over the other? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Fine-tuning** updates the *weights* of the LLM using domain-specific (question, answer) pairs. It is preferred when the **style, format, or behavior** of the model must change permanently (e.g., making a model speak Malayalam, follow a specific JSON schema).
- **RAG** does not modify weights; it injects *external context* into the prompt at inference time from a vector store. It is preferred when the **factual knowledge base changes frequently** (e.g., daily news, internal company policy docs) and retraining would be too expensive.
- **Decision rule:** If knowledge changes weekly, choose RAG. If the *task structure* is fixed but knowledge is static, choose fine-tuning. In production, the two are often **combined** (RAG over a fine-tuned model).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**Q: List any three major challenges in deploying Generative AI models at scale. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Compute Cost & Latency:** Autoregressive decoding is sequential and memory-bandwidth bound. Serving a 70B model to 10K concurrent users requires careful KV-cache management, batching, and quantization. Cost can exceed **\$1M/month** at scale.
2. **Hallucination & Factual Reliability:** LLMs generate fluent but factually incorrect text. Without RAG and verification layers, deploying them in healthcare, legal, or finance domains is unsafe.
3. **Safety, Bias & Prompt Injection:** Adversarial users can jailbreak the model (e.g., "DAN" prompts) to bypass safety guardrails. Robust deployment requires a separate *input classifier* and *output classifier* layer (e.g., Llama Guard).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4]**

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat diagram the end-to-end architecture of a production-grade Generative AI deployment. Identify and justify **four** non-functional requirements (NFRs) that govern the serving layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The architecture consists of the following components in sequence:
**Client → API Gateway → Auth/Rate Limiter → Preprocessor → RAG Retriever → Safety Filter → LLM Inference Engine → Postprocessor → Streaming Response → Observability.**

The four critical NFRs are:
1. **Latency (TTFT < 300 ms, decode > 30 tokens/sec):** Achieved via KV-cache reuse, continuous batching, and speculative decoding.
2. **Availability (99.9% SLA):** Achieved via multi-region active-active deployment, autoscaling groups on Kubernetes, and graceful degradation to smaller models.
3. **Throughput:** Achieved via PagedAttention (vLLM) and dynamic batching.
4. **Safety & Compliance:** Achieved via input/output classifiers, prompt-injection detectors, and audit logging.

---

**(b)** A fintech company is deploying a RAG-based customer-support chatbot. The system must answer queries about loan products using an internal policy document corpus. Describe **five** deployment challenges specific to this scenario and propose engineering mitigations for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **#** | **Challenge** | **Engineering Mitigation** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PII leakage** — chat history may contain Aadhaar, PAN, account numbers | On-prem deployment + regex-based PII redaction + Differential Privacy layer |
| 2 | **Hallucinated policy citations** — model invents clause numbers | Citation-grounded generation: prompt forces the model to cite retrieved chunk IDs and a separate verifier checks if the cited chunk exists |
| 3 | **Stale policy data** — RBI circulars update monthly | Nightly re-indexing of vector DB + freshness metadata in chunks |
| 4 | **Tone inconsistency with brand voice** | Fine-tune a 7B model on 5,000 curated historical support transcripts |
| 5 | **Cost per query exceeds margin** | Use distilled 3B model for tier-1 queries, escalate to larger model only for tier-2 complex queries; cache top 1,000 FAQs as exact-match |

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) *(Internal Choice Alternative)*
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4 Alternate]**

**Q: (a)** Derive the end-to-end latency formula for an autoregressive LLM call. Given a 7B parameter model on an A100 GPU with effective FLOPs utilization of 40%, prompt length 512 tokens, output length 256 tokens, compute (i) the prefill time, and (ii) the approximate end-to-end latency. Take peak FLOPs of A100 = 312 TFLOPs (FP16), hardware constant $k_1 = k_2 = 3$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $d$ hidden dimension for 7B ≈ 4096, $s_{\text{in}} = 512$, $s_{\text{out}} = 256$, $p_{\text{peak}} = 312$ TFLOPs, $U = 0.40$, so $p_{\text{eff}} = 124.8$ TFLOPs $= 1.248 \times 10^{14}$ FLOPs/s. Assume $t_{\text{queue}} \approx 0$.

**(i) Prefill time:**

$$ t_{\text{prefill}} = k_1 \cdot s_{\text{in}} \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{\text{eff}}^{-1} $$

$$ t_{\text{prefill}} = 3 \cdot 512 \cdot (4096)^2 \cdot (1.248 \times 10^{14})^{-1} $$

$$ t_{\text{prefill}} = 3 \cdot 512 \cdot 1.678 \times 10^7 \cdot 8.013 \times 10^{-15} $$

$$ t_{\text{prefill}} \approx 2.06 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{s} = 0.206 \; \text{ms} $$

**(ii) End-to-end latency:**

$$ T_{\text{E2E}} = t_{\text{prefill}} + s_{\text{out}} \cdot k_2 \cdot \left( s_{\text{in}} + \frac{s_{\text{out}}}{2} \right) \cdot d^2 \cdot p_{\text{eff}}^{-1} $$

$$ = 0.206 \times 10^{-3} + 256 \cdot 3 \cdot (512 + 128) \cdot (4096)^2 \cdot (1.248 \times 10^{14})^{-1} $$

$$ = 0.206 \times 10^{-3} + 256 \cdot 3 \cdot 640 \cdot 1.678 \times 10^7 \cdot 8.013 \times 10^{-15} $$

$$ = 0.206 \times 10^{-3} + 6.61 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{s} $$

$$ \boxed{\; T_{\text{E2E}} \approx 66.3 \; \text{ms} \;} $$

---

**(b)** List and briefly explain **six** major real-world use cases of Generative AI across different industries. For each use case, identify the most critical deployment challenge. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Code Generation (GitHub Copilot):** Critical challenge — *license compliance* (suggesting GPL code in proprietary codebase). Mitigation — license-aware retrieval.
2. **Drug Discovery (Insilico Medicine):** Critical challenge — *wet-lab validation cost* and *hallucinated molecules*. Mitigation — synthesizability filters (e.g., SA score, retrosynthesis analysis).
3. **Conversational Banking:** Critical challenge — *PII leakage*. Mitigation — on-prem + redaction.
4. **E-commerce Image Synthesis:** Critical challenge — *GPU cost*. Mitigation — distilled Stable Diffusion + LCM-LoRA for 4-step inference.
5. **Voice Cloning (ElevenLabs):** Critical challenge — *deepfake fraud*. Mitigation — watermarking, voice-liveness detection.
6. **Enterprise Document Q&A:** Critical challenge — *citation hallucination*. Mitigation — forced grounding + chunk-ID verification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing training-time compute with inference-time compute.** Students frequently write "GPU-hours" without specifying whether it is for *training* (offline, batch) or *inference* (online, latency-critical). Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this ambiguity.
> 2. **Writing RAG = LLM.** RAG is an *architecture*, not a *model*. The LLM is one component; the vector DB, retriever, and reranker are equally important.
> 3. **Forgetting non-functional requirements.** A common KTU marker requires you to mention at least **one NFR** (latency, cost, availability, safety). Answers that only describe *what the model does* lose 50% of marks.
> 4. **Mixing up FID and BLEU.** FID is for *images*, BLEU/ROUGE is for *text*. Examiners deduct for cross-domain metric confusion.
> 5. **Hallucinating framework names.** Stick to verified tools: *vLLM, LangChain, LlamaIndex, Pinecone, Weights & Biases, Langfuse*. Do not invent tool names.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 (KTU 2024):**

- **Deployment ≠ Training.** Always distinguish the *serving phase* from the *training phase* in answers.
- **Five Pillars:** Functional, Non-Functional, Economic, Trust/Safety, Observability.
- **RAG > Fine-tuning** for *frequently changing* knowledge; **Fine-tuning > RAG** for *fixed task structure*.
- **The KV-cache equation** $R_{\text{KV}} = 2 \cdot n_{\text{layers}} \cdot n_{\text{heads}} \cdot d_{\text{head}} \cdot s \cdot p$ dictates whether a model fits on one GPU.
- **The latency equation** $T_{\text{E2E}} = t_{\text{queue}} + t_{\text{prefill}} + s_{\text{out}} \cdot t_{\text{per-token}}$ must be derivable from scratch.
- **Six challenge tiers:** Compute, Quality, Safety, Regulatory, Operational, Economic.
- **FID** is for images (lower is better, typically < 10 for high quality). **BLEU/ROUGE** are for text.
- **Cost per 1K tokens** is the industry pricing unit; raw compute cost is **40–50× lower** than market price.
- **Production tools:** vLLM (serving), LangChain/LlamaIndex (orchestration), Pinecone/FAISS (vector DB), Langfuse/Phoenix (observability), Llama Guard (safety).
- **Real-world use cases** span: customer support, code gen, drug discovery, marketing, image synthesis, synthetic data, voice/music, enterprise search, robotics, education.
- **Regulatory:** GDPR (EU), DPDP Act 2023 (India), EU AI Act 2024, copyright lawsuits (NYT v. OpenAI).
